tableside: Angel's Envy finishes in ruby port wine casks, which gives it a sweetness and fruit character that makes it one of the easiest entry points on the list. Great for a guest who thinks they don't like bourbon.
story: Angel's Envy was founded in Louisville by master distiller Lincoln Henderson, who spent 40 years at Brown-Forman developing some of America's most celebrated bourbons before leaving to build something of his own. The port wine cask finish was his idea, inspired by Scotch finishing traditions applied to the American whiskey he knew better than almost anyone. He passed away in 2013, but his son Wes and the distillery he built in Louisville's NuLu neighborhood carry his vision forward. The name refers to the angel's share, the portion of whiskey lost to evaporation during aging, with the twist that what remains is so good the angels are envious.
trivia: Angel's Envy was one of the first American distilleries to popularize the cask finishing technique, which is now widespread across the industry.
Lincoln Henderson was the master distiller behind Woodford Reserve and Gentleman Jack before founding Angel's Envy in retirement.
The distillery on Main Street in Louisville is open for tours and is one of the most visited on the Urban Bourbon Trail.
mash_bill: 72% corn, 18% rye, 10% malted barley
why: Finished in port wine casks, which softens the rye spice and adds a distinctive red fruit sweetness. One of the most approachable high-rye bourbons on the market at any price.

tableside: Basil Hayden's is the lightest, most approachable bourbon on the list — lower proof, high-rye mash bill, easy drinking. Perfect for a guest who wants bourbon flavor without the weight.
story: Basil Hayden's is part of Jim Beam's Small Batch Collection, named after a distiller from the late 1700s who was known for using a higher percentage of rye in his mash bill than was typical for the era. That tradition carries through today , the Old Grand-Dad mash bill at 27% rye gives it a distinctive peppery note that sets it apart from sweeter, corn-heavy bourbons. At 80 proof it is deliberately accessible, designed for the guest who finds most bourbon too aggressive. It introduced a generation of wine drinkers to bourbon in the early 2000s and has held that position on serious lists ever since.
trivia: Basil Hayden Sr. was a Catholic Maryland farmer who led a group of 25 families to Kentucky in 1796, where he began distilling.
The high-rye mash bill used in Basil Hayden's is the same recipe used in Old Grand-Dad, another Beam product.
At 40% ABV, Basil Hayden's is one of the lowest proof bourbons on the list — a deliberate choice to maximize approachability.
mash_bill: 63% corn, 27% rye, 10% malted barley
why: Jim Beam's high-rye expression, using the Old Grand-Dad mash bill. One of the spiciest bourbons in its price range , the high rye content is immediately obvious.

name: 10 Year
tableside: The 10 Year takes Basil Hayden's approachable DNA and adds a decade of oak. Same gentle style, more complexity — a nice step up for someone who loves the standard but wants more depth.
story: Basil Hayden's 10 Year takes the brand's signature light, high-rye style and lets it develop over a full decade in new charred oak. Most Basil Hayden's expressions are released young and at low proof by design, so the 10 Year is a rare look at what the mash bill becomes with extended aging , more layered and complex without losing the approachability that defines the line. It's a limited release with modest production, making it one of the more underrated allocated bottles on the list relative to what's inside.
trivia: Ten years is considered a significant age statement for Kentucky bourbon, where the warm climate accelerates maturation compared to cooler Scottish conditions.
Despite the extra aging, Basil Hayden's 10 Year maintains the same 40% ABV as the standard expression — a deliberate choice to preserve the brand's lighter character.
Limited annual releases make this one of the harder Basil Hayden's expressions to find outside of allocated accounts.
mash_bill: 63% corn, 27% rye, 10% malted barley
why: The 10 year adds enough time in oak to round off the sharp edges of the high-rye mash bill without losing the spice that makes Basil Hayden's interesting.

name: 9 Year
tableside: Nine years old with a sherry cask finish — this is a bourbon for the guest who loves Scotch. Rich, fruity, and complex in a way that crosses the aisle between American and European whisky traditions.
story: Belle Meade is sourced from MGP's high-rye mash bills in Indiana and finished by Nashville's Nelson's Green Brier Distillery, a historic Tennessee distillery revived by brothers Charlie and Andy Nelson after over a century of dormancy. Their great-great-great-grandfather operated the original Green Brier Distillery in Tennessee before Prohibition shut it down in 1909. The 9 Year sherry cask finish adds dried fruit and European complexity that makes it stand out on any list. For a bourbon, it drinks unusually close to a Highlands Scotch, which makes it an excellent bridge bottle for guests who think they don't like bourbon.
trivia: Nelson's Green Brier was originally founded in the 1800s by Charles Nelson, whose descendants rediscovered the brand and relaunched it in 2014.
Sherry cask finishing adds esters and tannins from the fortified wine residue left in the wood, contributing dried fruit and nutty complexity.
Belle Meade is named after a historic antebellum plantation near Nashville that was famous for its thoroughbred horse breeding.
mash_bill: 64% corn, 30% rye, 6% malted barley
why: A blend of MGP's two high-rye bourbon mash bills, selected and aged by Nashville's Nelson's Green Brier Distillery. More rye character than most bourbons twice its price.

name: Pact #8
tableside: Blood Oath finishes in Calvados casks — French apple brandy barrels — which gives this a baked apple and orchard fruit character unlike anything else on the list. A great conversation piece.
story: Blood Oath is a limited annual release from Lux Row Distillers in Kentucky, created by master distiller and blender John Rempe, a certified food scientist who approaches whiskey blending with the precision of a chef developing a recipe. Each Pact sources aged bourbon from undisclosed Kentucky distilleries and applies an unusual cask finish. Pact 8 uses Calvados barrels, casks previously used to age French apple brandy from Normandy, which impart a distinctive orchard fruit character that sits on top of the classic bourbon base. Each release is numbered and once it is gone it is gone.
trivia: Calvados is an apple brandy produced in the Normandy region of France, aged in oak and subject to its own strict appellation rules.
Blood Oath releases a new Pact number each year, with a different finishing cask each time — making collecting the series a popular pursuit.
Lux Row Distillers, which produces Blood Oath, is located in Bardstown, Kentucky — often called the 'Bourbon Capital of the World.'
mash_bill: Undisclosed blend — sourced from multiple distilleries
why: Each annual Pact is a blend of bourbons from undisclosed sources, finished in a specific cask type. Pact 8 uses Calvados casks , apple brandy barrels from Normandy, France , adding a distinctive fruit character.

name: Pact #10
tableside: Finished in Cabernet Franc and Merlot casks, this one lands somewhere between a bourbon and a fine red wine barrel — dark fruit, a little tannic grip, genuinely interesting. Perfect for a wine-drinking guest who's curious about whiskey.
story: Pact 10 marks a decade of Blood Oath releases by master blender John Rempe, who has never disclosed the sources of his bourbon and never will. To mark the milestone he applied a double finish, first in Cabernet Franc barrels and then in Merlot casks, contributing dark berry esters and subtle tannins that don't normally appear in bourbon. The result is a profile that genuinely appeals to both bourbon drinkers and wine drinkers without alienating either. It's a deliberate crossover bottle built on real winemaking knowledge, not just a gimmick.
trivia: Cabernet Franc is a red wine grape grown widely in France's Loire Valley and Bordeaux, producing wines known for their herbal, dark fruit character.
Wine cask finishes are trickier than port or sherry finishes because wine barrels are thinner and impart flavor more quickly, requiring careful monitoring.
Each Blood Oath Pact is blended from bourbons of different ages and mash bills before finishing, giving the master blender more complexity to work with.
mash_bill: Undisclosed blend — sourced from multiple distilleries
why: Pact 10 marks a decade of releases with a double finish: Cabernet Franc barrels first, then Merlot casks. One of the more ambitious cask-finish bourbons released in 2024.

name: Pact 11
tableside: Añejo tequila barrels finish this one — agave sweetness, a hint of citrus, warm spice underneath the classic bourbon base. For the guest who drinks both bourbon and tequila, this is a fun meeting point.
story: Pact 11 takes the Blood Oath series in a direction no previous release has, using anejo tequila casks that previously held agave spirit aged at least one year in small oak barrels. The result is a bourbon with a distinctly Mexican-American character: agave sweetness and citrus lift sitting on top of Kentucky corn and oak. The blend itself combines 14-year ryed bourbon, 10-year wheated bourbon, and 7-year ryed bourbon, making it one of the more structurally complex releases in the series. It works because both spirits share a fondness for caramel and vanilla, with the tequila cask adding brightness rather than fighting the base.
trivia: Añejo tequila must be aged between one and three years in barrels no larger than 600 liters, concentrating flavors significantly before the wood is repurposed.
The agave plant used to make tequila takes 7–10 years to mature before harvest, making it one of the slowest raw materials in the spirits world.
Blood Oath Pact 11 is the first in the series to use a tequila cask, opening a new direction for future releases.
mash_bill: Undisclosed blend — sourced from multiple distilleries
why: Pact 11 finishes in Anejo tequila barrels , a relatively rare combination that adds citrus and agave notes to a blend of 14-year ryed, 10-year wheated, and 7-year ryed bourbon.

tableside: The gateway bourbon — same distillery as Pappy Van Winkle and Eagle Rare. At $17 a glass, one of the best values on the list. The fact that it's allocated at all tells you something about how sought-after this distillery is.
story: The Buffalo Trace Distillery in Frankfort, Kentucky is one of America's oldest continuously operating distilleries, established on a site where whiskey has been made since 1773 and surviving Prohibition by producing medicinal whiskey under government license. The distillery sits on the banks of the Kentucky River, on a path historically used by bison migrating north. Today it produces some of the most allocated bourbons in the world, including Pappy Van Winkle, George T. Stagg, Blanton's, and Eagle Rare, all from the same facility that makes this pour. Understanding Buffalo Trace is essential to understanding why so much of this list traces back to one address in Frankfort.
trivia: Buffalo Trace has operated continuously since 1787, surviving Prohibition by obtaining a license to produce whiskey for medicinal purposes — one of only a handful of distilleries to do so.
The distillery operated under many names — Ancient Age, George T. Stagg, and Schenley — before taking the Buffalo Trace name in 1999.
By law, bourbon must be made from at least 51% corn, aged in new charred oak containers, and entered into the barrel at no more than 125 proof.
mash_bill: ~75% corn, ~13% rye, ~12% malted barley (BT Mash Bill #1, undisclosed)
why: The flagship of one of America's most storied distilleries, established in 1773. Low-rye recipe produces a smooth, approachable bourbon that outperforms its price point consistently.

tableside: Bulleit is the high-rye workhorse — dry, spicy, and versatile. At $11 it's the most approachable price point on the bourbon list, and the spice makes it genuinely interesting neat or on the rocks.
story: Bulleit Bourbon is built around one of the highest rye percentages of any widely available bourbon at 28%, which gives it the dry, spicy character the brand is known for. Tom Bulleit revived a family recipe in 1987 attributed to his ancestor Augustus Bulleit, a Louisville distiller from the mid-1800s who disappeared without explanation while transporting a shipment of whiskey to New Orleans. Today Bulleit is distilled by Four Roses in Lawrenceburg under a supply agreement, and the high-rye mash bill remains unchanged. The frontier-style bottle is deliberate, designed to stand out on a back bar from across the room.
trivia: Bulleit's mash bill contains nearly twice the rye of most Kentucky bourbons, which is why it tastes significantly drier and spicier than corn-dominant expressions.
Augustus Bulleit reportedly disappeared while transporting a shipment of whiskey from Louisville to New Orleans in the 1860s — his fate was never determined.
Bulleit is produced at the Shelby County Distillery in Shelbyville, Kentucky, a Diageo facility that also produces other major brands.
mash_bill: 68% corn, 28% rye, 4% malted barley
why: One of the highest rye percentages of any widely available bourbon at 28%. Distinctive spice-forward character that makes it one of the most mixable and sippable bourbons on the market.

name: 10 Year
tableside: Eagle Rare is the sleeper on the list — ten years old, Buffalo Trace distillery, $20 a glass. It consistently punches above its price point and is one of the most allocated bottles we carry. If it's available, order it.
story: Eagle Rare 10 Year is produced at Buffalo Trace and is one of the most consistently praised bourbons at any price point. Aged a minimum of ten years in new charred oak, it develops a complexity , dried herbs, orange peel, leather , that most bourbons twice its price can't match. Its allocated status is a function of Buffalo Trace's production constraints rather than any artificial scarcity: there simply isn't enough of it to meet demand. When it's on the list, it's worth pointing out to any guest who appreciates value.
trivia: Eagle Rare was originally produced by Seagram's in the 1970s before Buffalo Trace acquired the brand and moved production to Frankfort.
The 'single barrel' designation on some Eagle Rare bottles means every bottle in that batch came from one specific aging barrel, meaning no two batches taste exactly alike.
Eagle Rare consistently wins awards at international spirits competitions, often beating expressions that cost three to five times as much.
mash_bill: ~75% corn, ~13% rye, ~12% malted barley (BT Mash Bill #1, undisclosed)
why: Same mash bill as Buffalo Trace, aged longer in a different warehouse. A single barrel expression that consistently earns its reputation as one of the best bourbons under $40.

name: Small Batch
tableside: E.H. Taylor is Bottled in Bond — 100 proof, one distillery, one season, four years minimum. It's the federal government's original quality guarantee for bourbon, and Taylor Small Batch delivers on it at $20.
story: Colonel Edmund Haynes Taylor Jr. is one of the most important figures in bourbon history , he lobbied for the Bottled-in-Bond Act of 1897, which established the first federal quality standards for American whiskey. The E.H. Taylor line from Buffalo Trace is a tribute to his legacy, and every expression carries the Bottled-in-Bond designation he fought for: 100 proof, produced at a single distillery in a single distilling season, aged at least four years in a federally bonded warehouse. The Small Batch is the entry point to the line and one of the best allocated deals on the list.
trivia: The Bottled-in-Bond Act of 1897 was passed partly in response to widespread adulteration of whiskey — producers were adding coloring, flavoring, and even dangerous chemicals to boost profits.
E.H. Taylor Jr. also built the Old Taylor Distillery, now known as Castle & Key, which was restored and reopened in 2018.
At 100 proof (50% ABV), Bottled-in-Bond bourbons often show more intensity and complexity than lower-proof expressions of similar age.
mash_bill: ~75% corn, ~13% rye, ~12% malted barley (BT Mash Bill #1, undisclosed)
why: Named after the man who built Buffalo Trace Distillery. The Small Batch is the most accessible expression in the Colonel Taylor line , same low-rye mash bill as Buffalo Trace, with more time in oak.

name: 90th Anniversary
tableside: A commemorative release from one of Kentucky's great family-owned distilleries — nearly a decade old, barrel proof strength, serious depth. The 90th anniversary edition marks a milestone for a distillery that survived fire, Prohibition, and everything in between.
story: Heaven Hill is the largest independent family-owned distillery in the United States, founded in 1935 by the Shapira family shortly after Prohibition ended. The distillery has survived extraordinary adversity , including a catastrophic fire in 1996 that destroyed warehouses holding nearly 90,000 barrels , and remained family-owned through it all. The 90th Anniversary release celebrates that history with a high-proof, well-aged expression that reflects the depth of their aging inventory. Heaven Hill produces more bourbon than almost any other distillery, but releases like this one show what they hold back for special occasions.
trivia: The 1996 Heaven Hill fire was one of the largest bourbon-related disasters in history — burning whiskey flowed into nearby Bardstown roads and caught fire, creating rivers of flame.
Heaven Hill owns more aging bourbon than almost any other producer, with warehouses holding millions of barrels across Bardstown.
The Shapira family has operated Heaven Hill for over 85 years without ever selling to a corporate parent — a rarity in the consolidated spirits industry.
mash_bill: 78% corn, 10% rye, 12% malted barley
why: A rare commemorative release from Heaven Hill celebrating the distillery's 90th anniversary. Uses the same standard Heaven Hill mash bill but with exceptional barrel selection.

name: Cask Collection
tableside: High West finishes this in vermouth casks — which is unusual and genuinely interesting. You get the botanical, herbal notes of vermouth on top of a solid blended bourbon base. For a guest who loves cocktails, this is the neat version of a Manhattan.
story: High West Distillery in Park City, Utah was one of the first craft distilleries to make a name for itself through exceptional sourcing and blending rather than waiting decades to age its own stock. The Cask Collection series applies unusual finishing casks , including vermouth barrels , to already complex blended bourbons and ryes. The vermouth finish is inspired by the Manhattan cocktail, bringing the botanical, herbal character of the aperitif into the whiskey itself. It's a creative approach that respects both traditions.
trivia: High West was the first distillery to legally operate in Utah since 1870, opening in 2006 in a converted livery stable in Park City.
Vermouth barrels retain botanicals and wine residue that impart herbal, floral notes distinctly different from wine or spirit cask finishes.
High West was acquired by Constellation Brands in 2016 but continues to operate with the same blending philosophy established by founder David Perkins.
mash_bill: Blend of MGP bourbons: 75% corn, 21% rye, 4% malted barley
why: High West blends whiskeys the way a chef combines ingredients , this cask collection expression changes annually based on what master blender Brendan Coyle finds worth showcasing.

name: Private Stock
tableside: Johnnie Drum Private Stock is the hidden gem of the allocated list — $17 for a well-aged, full-flavored Kentucky bourbon that most guests have never heard of. Easy to recommend when the more famous allocated bottles aren't available.
story: Johnnie Drum is produced by Heaven Hill and has been around since the 1970s, though it remains relatively obscure compared to the distillery's more famous labels. The Private Stock expression is bottled at a higher proof than the standard release, giving it more intensity and depth while maintaining Heaven Hill's signature approachable caramel and oak profile. Its allocated status is somewhat surprising given its modest price , a reflection of genuine demand from those who've discovered it rather than hype-driven scarcity.
trivia: Johnnie Drum has been produced at Heaven Hill since the 1970s but has never received the marketing investment of the distillery's more prominent brands.
Heaven Hill's house style — rich caramel, approachable sweetness, good oak — runs consistently across its many labels from Elijah Craig to Evan Williams to Johnnie Drum.
At $17, Johnnie Drum Private Stock represents one of the best value propositions among allocated bourbons anywhere in Virginia.
mash_bill: 75% corn, 13% rye, 12% malted barley
why: A Kentucky bourbon named after a former owner of Heaven Hill Distillery. Private Stock is aged a minimum of 15 years, making it one of the longest-aged bourbons at its price point.

name: Cigar Blend
detail: Washington DC · 57.73% · Cognac, Armagnac & Sherry Finish
tableside: Joseph Magnus is a DC distillery, and the Cigar Blend is their showpiece — finished in Cognac, Armagnac, and Sherry casks simultaneously. Rich, complex, and high proof. It's designed to stand up to a cigar, which means it stands up to anything.
story: Joseph Magnus & Co. was founded in Cincinnati in the 1890s and was one of America's great whiskey brands before Prohibition shut it down. The brand was revived in Washington DC by Brett Pontoni and his son Sam, who began finishing sourced high-quality bourbon in European casks before transitioning to their own distillate. The Cigar Blend uses a triple finish , Cognac, Armagnac, and Sherry casks , to build a profile specifically designed to complement tobacco. It's among the most complex bourbons produced on the East Coast.
trivia: Joseph Magnus & Co. was one of the first American distilleries to bottle its own straight whiskey, rather than selling barrels to blenders — a practice that was unusual in the 1890s.
Armagnac is a French brandy from Gascony, older than Cognac and known for its deeper, earthier character due to continuous-still distillation.
Triple cask finishing — using three different barrel types simultaneously or sequentially — is rare in bourbon production and requires careful blending to avoid any one flavor dominating.
mash_bill: Undisclosed MGP sourced blend
why: Sourced from MGP in Indiana and finished in a combination of Oloroso sherry, Pedro Ximenez sherry, and cognac casks , a triple finish that is extremely rare in American bourbon.

name: Murray Hill Club
tableside: The Murray Hill Club is Joseph Magnus's blended expression — more approachable than the Cigar Blend but still finished in European casks. Named after the DC neighborhood where Magnus had his office, it's a great introduction to what this distillery does.
story: Murray Hill Club is named after the Washington DC neighborhood where Joseph Magnus maintained his offices in the early 1900s. The expression blends bourbons of different ages and mashbills before finishing in select European casks, aiming for balance and approachability rather than the intensity of the Cigar Blend. It represents the Magnus philosophy of using great source material and thoughtful finishing to create something more than the sum of its parts , a blending tradition with deep roots in both American and European whisky culture.
trivia: Murray Hill is a neighborhood in Northwest Washington DC that was home to many prominent businesses and residences in the early twentieth century.

Blended bourbon — combining bourbons of different ages, mashbills, or distilleries — is a legitimate and skilled craft, though it carries less prestige than single barrel expressions in current market culture.
Joseph Magnus sources its base whiskey from MGP in Indiana, one of the most respected contract distilleries in the US, before applying its finishing program.
mash_bill: Undisclosed MGP sourced blend
why: A recreation of the pre-Prohibition Murray Hill Club whiskey using the same techniques Joseph Magnus used over a century ago. A blend of 18-year, 11-year, and 9-year whiskeys.

name: 9 Year
tableside: Knob Creek 9 Year is the backbone of the list — nine years old, 100 proof, $13 a glass. It's the bourbon you reach for when a guest wants something serious without the allocated price tag.
story: Knob Creek is part of Jim Beam's Small Batch Collection, named after the creek near Abraham Lincoln's boyhood home in Kentucky. The 9 Year was created in 1992 by Booker Noe, Jim Beam's grandson and a legendary figure in the industry who also created Booker's Bourbon and Baker's. His philosophy was straightforward: age it longer, bottle it at full 100 proof, and price it to be the best value serious bourbon available. Master distiller Fred Noe, Booker's son, continues that standard today and has never changed the formula.
trivia: Knob Creek was temporarily discontinued in 2009 due to overwhelming demand depleting the aged supply — a shortage that became known as the 'bourbon drought.'
Fred Noe, who oversees Knob Creek, is the great-grandson of James B. Beam, making him the seventh generation of the Beam family in the whiskey business.
At 100 proof and 9 years old, Knob Creek represents a sweet spot in bourbon production where age and proof work together to maximize complexity without harshness.
mash_bill: 75% corn, 13% rye, 12% malted barley
why: Jim Beam's premium small batch expression aged 9 years. Uses four times more malted barley than the standard Jim Beam recipe, producing a noticeably richer mouthfeel.

name: 12 Year
tableside: Three more years in the barrel takes Knob Creek somewhere noticeably darker and richer. At 12 years the oak is more present, the sweetness has deepened, and the whole thing has a weight that the 9 Year doesn't quite reach.
story: Knob Creek 12 Year adds three additional years of Kentucky aging to the classic 9 Year, pushing the oak and caramel character further without losing the bold, high-proof character Booker Noe designed the brand around. The result is a noticeably darker and more complex whiskey that rewards guests who already know the original and want to understand what more time in the barrel actually does. Its relative scarcity is a function of simple production math: pulling barrels at 12 years means fewer bottles from the same warehouses.
trivia: Each additional year of Kentucky aging extracts more oak tannins and compounds from the barrel, darkening the color and adding complexity — but also the risk of over-oaking if left too long.
The allocated status of Knob Creek 12 Year is partly a function of math: aging three more years reduces yield as barrels shrink from evaporation, the 'angel's share.'
Knob Creek 12 Year is bottled at the same 100 proof as the 9 Year, allowing direct comparison of how time alone changes the spirit.
mash_bill: 75% corn, 13% rye, 12% malted barley
why: Knob Creek aged three additional years beyond the 9 Year. The extra time produces more developed oak and dried fruit notes without losing the bold, full-proof character the brand is known for.

tableside: Maker's Mark is the wheated bourbon standard — soft, sweet, approachable. If a guest isn't sure what they want, this is never a wrong answer. The red wax seal is one of the most recognized symbols in American whiskey.
story: Maker's Mark was created in the 1950s by Bill Samuels Sr., who famously burned his family's 170-year-old bourbon recipe and started from scratch. His innovation was replacing rye with red winter wheat, producing a softer, sweeter bourbon that stood apart from every other expression on the market at the time. His wife Margie designed the iconic square bottle and hand-dipped red wax seal, making it one of the most recognizable bottles in spirits. The Loretto, Kentucky distillery is a National Historic Landmark, one of the most visited in the state, and has been making the same wheated recipe without interruption for nearly 70 years.
trivia: Bill Samuels Sr. literally burned his family's ancestral bourbon recipe when he decided to create Maker's Mark — a dramatic break with tradition that defined the brand's identity.
The red wax dipping that seals every Maker's Mark bottle is still done by hand at the distillery, making each bottle slightly unique.
Maker's Mark was the first bourbon distillery to be designated a National Historic Landmark by the US Department of the Interior, in 1980.
mash_bill: 70% corn, 16% winter wheat, 14% malted barley
why: One of the original wheated bourbons , Maker's replaced rye with red winter wheat in 1958, producing a softer, rounder spirit. The signature red wax dip is done by hand at the distillery.

name: BEP 2023
tableside: BEP stands for Batch Proof — this is Maker's Mark at full barrel strength, no water added. The lower entry proof experiment means the whiskey entered the barrel at lower strength, extracting wood differently. The result is noticeably richer and more intense than standard Maker's.
story: Maker's Mark BEP 2023 is an experiment in distillery craft: by entering the spirit into the barrel at a lower proof than standard practice (which uses up to 125 proof entry), the whiskey interacts with the wood differently, extracting flavors at a different rate and balance. Released at full barrel strength without dilution, BEP shows what that lower-entry approach produces , a fuller, richer Maker's Mark that maintains the wheated sweetness the brand is known for while adding intensity that the standard expression doesn't have. It's a fascinating comparison piece for any Maker's fan.
trivia: Federal law allows bourbon to be entered into the barrel at up to 125 proof (62.5% ABV) — lower entry proof means more water in the barrel from the start, changing the extraction dynamics.
Batch Proof releases are bottled without chill filtration or dilution, preserving every compound that developed during aging.
The BEP experiment is part of Maker's Mark's broader Wood Finishing Series program, which explores how different barrel treatments change their wheated bourbon base.
mash_bill: 70% corn, 16% winter wheat, 14% malted barley
why: BEP stands for Bourbon Enthusiast Program , a bottling done in collaboration with the Maker's Mark Ambassador program, using the same wheated mash bill at a slightly different proof point.

name: Cellar Aged 2024
tableside: The oldest Maker's Mark on the list — a blend of 12 and 13 year barrels, bottled at full strength. This is what happens when you give a wheated bourbon time to really develop. Soft sweetness has turned into something much more serious.
story: Maker's Mark Cellar Aged 2024 represents the outer edge of what the distillery's wheated mashbill can become with extended aging. Blending barrels at 12 and 13 years , well beyond the typical Maker's aging cycle , produces a whiskey that retains the brand's signature soft sweetness while developing oak complexity and intensity that the standard expression never reaches. Released at full barrel strength without dilution, it's a limited annual release that gives longtime Maker's fans a reason to look at the brand with fresh eyes.
trivia: Wheat as a flavoring grain in bourbon produces softer, sweeter whiskey than rye, but it also develops differently with extended aging — becoming richer and more complex rather than spicier.
At nearly 60% ABV, Cellar Aged 2024 is among the highest proof expressions in the Maker's Mark portfolio, a significant departure from the brand's historically approachable positioning.
The 'cellar aged' designation refers to barrels stored in specific warehouse locations that age more slowly, allowing the distillery to build older stock without over-oaking.
mash_bill: 70% corn, 16% winter wheat, 14% malted barley
why: An extended cellar-aging expression that spends additional time in climate-controlled storage after barrel maturation. One of the more experimental releases in the Maker's Mark lineup.

name: Small Batch
tableside: Michter's Small Batch is a reliable, well-made Kentucky bourbon at an honest price point. Not flashy, but consistently good — the kind of pour that makes a guest feel well taken care of without spending a lot.
story: Michter's claims roots going back to 1753 in Pennsylvania, though the modern brand was relaunched in Louisville in the 1990s after a period of dormancy. Today it operates out of a historic fort distillery site in Louisville and sources some of its whiskey while building its own aged inventory. The Small Batch expression is their flagship , approachable, well-balanced, and representing the house style of caramel-forward bourbon with restrained spice. It's a brand that has grown significantly in prestige over the past decade.
trivia: Michter's claims to have supplied whiskey to George Washington's troops at Valley Forge in 1777, though the historical record on this is debated by bourbon historians.
The Fort Nelson Distillery in Louisville, which Michter's restored and opened in 2019, is housed in a building listed on the National Register of Historic Places.
Michter's ages its barrels in climate-controlled warehouses, a deliberate choice to slow maturation and develop more consistent flavor across batches.
mash_bill: Undisclosed — Kentucky rye bourbon, approximately 79% corn, 11% rye, 10% malted barley
why: Michter's uses a low barrel entry proof of 103, significantly lower than the industry standard of 125. Lower entry proof means more flavor character retained from the grain in the finished whiskey.

name: Toasted Barrel Finish
tableside: After standard aging, Michter's puts this in a second barrel — toasted but not charred — which extracts different wood compounds and adds a deeper, sweeter layer. It's a subtle but noticeable step up from the Small Batch.
story: Michter's Toasted Barrel Finish takes already mature Small Batch bourbon and finishes it in a second, custom-toasted barrel , a barrel that has been heated to bring out wood sugars without the heavy char of a standard bourbon barrel. Toasting versus charring extracts fundamentally different compounds: char filters and adds smoke, while toast caramelizes wood sugars and adds sweetness and vanilla. The result is a noticeably sweeter, more dessert-like bourbon that appeals to guests who find most bourbon too dry or aggressive.
trivia: The difference between toasting and charring a barrel is temperature and time — toasting caramelizes wood sugars at lower heat, while charring burns the wood surface to create a carbon filter layer.
Double-barreled finishing was pioneered by producers looking to add complexity without waiting additional years in standard barrels — a time-efficient way to develop new flavor profiles.
Michter's uses custom-specified barrels from independent cooperages, allowing them to control toast and char levels more precisely than most producers.
mash_bill: Undisclosed — Kentucky rye bourbon, approximately 79% corn, 11% rye, 10% malted barley
why: The standard Michter's Small Batch, but after primary aging the bourbon is placed in a second, freshly toasted barrel. The toasting , not charring , extracts different flavor compounds from the wood.

tableside: Old Charter Oak ages in barrels made from a specific American oak species — 'finest oak' — which imparts a distinctly different wood character than standard white oak. For a guest who loves the oak note in bourbon, this is worth trying.
story: Old Charter Oak is a Buffalo Trace expression that experiments with barrel wood species , using Chinkapin, French, Canadian, and other oak varieties rather than the standard American white oak that defines most bourbon. Each release specifies the wood type, allowing consumers to taste how dramatically cooperage choices affect the final spirit. The 'Finest Oak' designation refers to a specific high-quality American oak selection that produces a cleaner, more delicate wood influence than standard cooperage. It's a fascinating study in how much the barrel shapes the bourbon.
trivia: American white oak (Quercus alba) is the dominant barrel wood for bourbon, but different species impart dramatically different tannin structures, aromatic compounds, and flavor profiles.
Chinkapin oak, used in some Old Charter Oak releases, produces higher levels of certain wood sugars than white oak, contributing noticeable sweetness.
Buffalo Trace's experimental releases — including Old Charter Oak — are part of their Experimental Collection program, one of the most ambitious research projects in American whiskey.
mash_bill: ~75% corn, ~13% rye, ~12% malted barley (BT Mash Bill #1, undisclosed)
why: Part of the Buffalo Trace portfolio, aged in different oak varieties depending on the release. The Oak expression uses American oak from different tree species to explore how wood type changes flavor.

name: 9 Year
tableside: Old Fitzgerald is a wheated Bottled-in-Bond bourbon from Heaven Hill — softer mashbill, 100 proof, nine years old. Released in limited decanters twice a year, it's one of the more sought-after wheated bourbons on the allocated market.
story: Old Fitzgerald has a storied history , it was one of the most prestigious bourbon brands of the mid-twentieth century, known for its wheated mashbill at a time when most bourbon used rye. Heaven Hill acquired the brand and revived it as a premium allocated release in 2018, using the original wheated recipe and presenting it in ornate decanter bottles that reference the brand's historic packaging. The Bottled-in-Bond designation adds another layer of legitimacy: 100 proof, one distillery, one season, government oversight.
trivia: Old Fitzgerald was originally produced by the Stitzel-Weller Distillery in Louisville, which was also responsible for Pappy Van Winkle and W.L. Weller — all wheated bourbons.
Heaven Hill releases Old Fitzgerald twice a year in Spring and Fall, with each release featuring a different age statement and decanter design.
The wheated mashbill used in Old Fitzgerald produces a softer, more approachable spirit than rye-forward bourbons, making it popular with guests who find most bourbon too aggressive.
mash_bill: 68% corn, 20% wheat, 12% malted barley (Heaven Hill wheated)
why: Old Fitzgerald is one of America's oldest wheated bourbon brands, originally from the legendary Stitzel-Weller Distillery. Heaven Hill acquired the brand and has revived it as a premium bottled-in-bond release.

name: 11 Year
tableside: Two more years on the 9 Year brings more oak and depth while keeping the wheated softness. At the same price, the 11 Year is worth suggesting to anyone who ordered the 9 Year and wants to compare — the difference is meaningful.
story: Old Fitzgerald 11 Year is released alongside the 9 Year as part of Heaven Hill's semi-annual decanter series, giving drinkers a direct age comparison within the same brand. The additional two years in Kentucky's climate push the oak contribution further, deepening the color and adding layers of dried fruit and spice that the younger expression doesn't quite reach. Both share the same wheated mashbill and Bottled-in-Bond credentials, making side-by-side comparison an educational experience for staff and guests alike.
trivia: Aging bourbon in Kentucky for 11 years exposes each barrel to roughly 11 cycles of seasonal temperature change, each cycle driving whiskey deeper into and out of the wood.
The ornate decanter bottles used for Old Fitzgerald are designed to reference the brand's mid-century packaging, when decorative decanters were a premium bourbon presentation standard.
Heaven Hill's aging warehouses in Bardstown, Kentucky hold some of the oldest and most valuable bourbon stocks in the industry — Old Fitzgerald draws from that deep inventory.
mash_bill: 68% corn, 20% wheat, 12% malted barley (Heaven Hill wheated)
why: Two additional years in barrel beyond the 9 Year, increasing the oak influence and complexity. Old Fitzgerald bottled-in-bond releases are among the most anticipated annual wheated bourbon releases.

tableside: Old Forester 1897 is Bottled-in-Bond at $17 — one of the best value pours on the list. Named for the year the BIB standard was established, it's a deliberate tribute to the federal quality guarantee that defined American whiskey.
story: Old Forester is America's oldest continuously produced bourbon , Brown-Forman has made it without interruption since 1870, including through Prohibition when they held one of the few licenses to produce medicinal whiskey. The 1897 expression is named for the year the Bottled-in-Bond Act was passed, and carries that designation as a point of pride: 100 proof, single distillery, single season, government bonded. At $17 it's an exceptional value for an authenticated, well-made Kentucky bourbon with deep historical roots.
trivia: Old Forester was the first bourbon to be sold exclusively in sealed glass bottles, a practice George Garvin Brown began in 1870 to guarantee consistency and prevent adulteration.
Brown-Forman, which produces Old Forester, is one of the few remaining family-controlled companies in the Fortune 500 — the Brown family has maintained control since the company's founding.
The Bottled-in-Bond Act of 1897, which the 1897 expression commemorates, was the first federal consumer protection law for food and drink in US history.
mash_bill: 72% corn, 18% rye, 10% malted barley
why: The 1897 commemorates the year Old Forester first introduced bottled-in-bond standards. It remains one of the best value bottled-in-bond bourbons available today.

tableside: 1924 is Old Forester's 10 year expression — named for the year Prohibition ended Brown-Forman's medicinal license. A decade in the barrel takes this well beyond the standard Old Forester, with noticeably more oak and depth.
story: Old Forester 1924 is named for the year Prohibition effectively ended Brown-Forman's ability to produce whiskey for medicinal sale, forcing a pause in what had been an unbroken run of production since 1870. The 10 year age statement represents the most patient expression in the Old Forester Whiskey Row series, producing a bourbon with the depth and developed oak character that only time in Kentucky's climate can create. Brown-Forman's historic Shively distillery produces the spirit from the same mash bill used across the Old Forester lineup, with aging and barrel selection doing the heavy lifting.
trivia: During Prohibition, Brown-Forman held one of only six licenses issued to distilleries to produce whiskey for medicinal purposes — a significant competitive advantage when it ended.
Old Forester's Whiskey Row series is named for the block of Main Street in Louisville where many historic bourbon producers had their offices in the late 1800s.
At 10 years old and 100 proof, the 1924 sits in a sweet spot for Kentucky bourbon aging — old enough for real complexity, young enough to avoid over-oaking.
mash_bill: 72% corn, 18% rye, 10% malted barley
why: The 1924 is Old Forester's most premium regular expression, named for the year master distiller William LaRue Weller joined the company. Uses the same Brown-Forman mash bill aged to full maturity.

name: Birthday Bourbon 2025
tableside: Birthday Bourbon is Old Forester's annual limited release — 12 years old, from a single day's production. Each year is slightly different depending on the barrels selected. At $60 it's the most expensive Old Forester on the list and worth every dollar.
story: Old Forester Birthday Bourbon is released annually on September 2nd, George Garvin Brown's birthday, and is made from a single day's production , a tradition that dates back to 1987 and predates most of the current annual limited release craze in bourbon. Each vintage is aged 12 years and selected from specific warehouse locations, meaning the flavor profile shifts subtly from year to year. The 2025 release represents 12 years of patient aging from barrels laid down in 2013, making it a genuine time capsule from a specific moment in the distillery's history.
trivia: Old Forester Birthday Bourbon was one of the first annual limited releases in American bourbon — launched in 1987, decades before the practice became widespread.
The September 2nd release date honors George Garvin Brown, who founded Brown-Forman and began producing Old Forester in 1870.
Each Birthday Bourbon vintage is selected from a single day's production, meaning every bottle shares the same distillation date — an unusual level of specificity in bourbon provenance.
mash_bill: 72% corn, 18% rye, 10% malted barley
why: Released annually on September 2nd, the Birthday Bourbon is made from barrels hand-selected by Old Forester's master distiller. No two years taste the same.

name: Rio
tableside: Amburana is a Brazilian wood used in cachaça production — finishing bourbon in it adds cinnamon, warm spice, and a tropical quality you won't find anywhere else on this list. Penelope Rio is genuinely unlike anything else we carry.
story: Penelope Bourbon is a Louisville-based brand founded by Mike Dedovich, named after his daughter. The Rio expression uses a double finish: first in American honey barrels, then in Amburana wood, a Brazilian timber used traditionally in the production of cachaça. Amburana is extraordinarily aromatic , it imparts cinnamon, clove, and tropical notes that are entirely foreign to the American whiskey tradition. The result is a bourbon that tastes like nothing else on any list, which makes it a compelling recommendation for guests who claim to have tried everything.
trivia: Amburana (Amburana cearensis) is a tropical hardwood native to South America, used for centuries in Brazilian spirit production for its intense aromatic compounds.
Penelope Bourbon was founded in 2019 and grew rapidly on the strength of its innovative finishing program, which includes several unusual wood and barrel combinations.
The combination of honey barrel and Amburana finishing is designed to layer sweetness — the honey barrel adds sugar compounds, while Amburana adds spice and exotic wood character.
mash_bill: Undisclosed sourced blend — MGP high rye recipe
why: Penelope is a Washington DC-based brand that sources and finishes bourbon. The Rio expression is finished in Oloroso sherry casks, adding a rich dried fruit sweetness to the MGP base.

name: Special Reserve 12 Year
tableside: Pappy Van Winkle's family reserve — the 12 year is the most accessible expression in the Van Winkle line, and at $90 a glass it's still a rare opportunity. Wheated mashbill, Buffalo Trace distillery, and one of the most recognized names in American whiskey.
story: The Van Winkle family has been associated with fine Kentucky bourbon for over a century. Julian 'Pappy' Van Winkle Sr. ran the Stitzel-Weller Distillery in Louisville for decades, producing wheated bourbons that became legendary. After the distillery closed in 1992, his grandson Julian Van Winkle III partnered with Buffalo Trace to continue production, using the family's original wheated mashbill. Today, Van Winkle releases are among the most sought-after spirits in the world , the 12 Year is the most approachable entry point into the family's lineup.
trivia: Pappy Van Winkle's Family Reserve releases are allocated by a lottery system in many states, with some bottles selling for thousands of dollars on the secondary market.
The Van Winkle wheated mashbill — using wheat instead of rye as the secondary grain — is the same recipe used for W.L. Weller and Old Fitzgerald, all descendants of the Stitzel-Weller tradition.
Julian Van Winkle III is said to have told his distillery workers that he wanted to make bourbon 'the old fashioned, time-honored way' — a philosophy that prioritizes aging over volume.
mash_bill: ~76% corn, ~20% wheat, ~4% malted barley (BT wheated mash bill, undisclosed)
why: The Van Winkle Special Reserve Lot B is considered the most accessible entry point into the Pappy Van Winkle family. Wheated mash bill, 12 years old, and among the most sought-after bourbons in America.

name: Decadence
tableside: Widow Jane finishes their 10 year bourbon in maple syrup barrels — which sounds like a gimmick but genuinely works. The maple adds sweetness and a distinct character without being cloying. Great for a guest with a sweet tooth who still wants complexity.
story: Widow Jane Distillery in Red Hook, Brooklyn draws its water from the Rosendale mine in upstate New York , a limestone aquifer that the distillery believes produces a distinctly clean, mineral-free water for dilution. The Decadence expression takes their 10 year aged bourbon and finishes it in barrels that previously held pure maple syrup, adding a natural sweetness and complexity that sets it apart from other finished bourbons. It's a Brooklyn-made product with genuine craft behind it, not just an interesting label.
trivia: Widow Jane uses water from the Rosendale limestone mines in New York, which provided cement for the Brooklyn Bridge and the base of the Statue of Liberty — the distillery considers the mineral content a defining quality factor.
Maple syrup barrel finishing is rare in bourbon production — most finished bourbons use wine, spirit, or beer barrels rather than food-grade commodity barrels.
The Widow Jane name references the Widow Jane Mine in Rosendale, New York, from which the distillery sources its water.
mash_bill: Undisclosed — sourced from multiple distilleries
why: An annual limited release from Brooklyn's Widow Jane Distillery. Decade-old bourbon from Indiana, Kentucky, and Tennessee is finished in Crown Maple syrup barrels from the Hudson Valley.

name: Special Reserve
tableside: Weller Special Reserve is the entry point to the Weller wheated bourbon family — same mashbill as Pappy Van Winkle, same Buffalo Trace distillery, fraction of the price. The most talked-about value pour in American whiskey right now.
story: W.L. Weller is named after William Larue Weller, a 19th century Louisville whiskey merchant credited with popularizing wheated bourbon. Today, Weller is produced at Buffalo Trace using the same wheated mashbill as Pappy Van Winkle , a fact that has made the entire Weller line intensely sought-after as an accessible alternative to Pappy's near-impossible allocation. Special Reserve is the entry expression: approachable, soft, and genuinely good bourbon at a price that still surprises people who know what they're drinking.
trivia: W.L. Weller and Pappy Van Winkle share the same wheated mashbill and are produced at the same distillery — Buffalo Trace — making Weller the closest most drinkers will ever get to the Van Winkle experience.
William Larue Weller is believed to have been among the first distillers to use wheat instead of rye as the secondary grain in bourbon, establishing the wheated style in the 1840s.
Despite being allocated, Weller Special Reserve is one of the lower-priced allocated bourbons in the state — a function of Buffalo Trace's commitment to keeping entry-level expressions accessible.
mash_bill: ~76% corn, ~20% wheat, ~4% malted barley (BT wheated mash bill, undisclosed)
why: The entry point into the Weller wheated bourbon family. Made from the same mash bill as Pappy Van Winkle, aged shorter. One of the most sought-after bourbons in America relative to its retail price.

name: C.Y.B.P.
tableside: C.Y.B.P. stands for Craft Your Bourbon Profile — the recipe was developed through crowdsourcing, letting consumers vote on mashbill, aging, and proof. The result is a wheated bourbon dialed in by the people who drink it most.
story: Weller C.Y.B.P. stands for Craft Your Bourbon Profile, an unusual experiment in which Buffalo Trace allowed Weller enthusiasts to vote on specific production decisions, from proof adjustments to barrel selection criteria. The result is a bourbon shaped directly by its most engaged drinkers rather than a master distiller working alone, which gives it a different kind of authenticity. It retains the Weller wheated character while reflecting a community's collective preference for balance and approachability. As an allocated Buffalo Trace wheater it carries the same demand as the rest of the Weller lineup.
trivia: The crowdsourcing approach used for C.Y.B.P. is rare in bourbon production, where recipes are typically closely guarded and developed exclusively by distillery staff.
Buffalo Trace collected input from tens of thousands of Weller enthusiasts during the C.Y.B.P. development process, making it one of the most democratically produced bourbons in history.
The 47.5% ABV proof point was selected through the crowdsourcing process — slightly above the standard Weller Special Reserve but below barrel proof, reflecting a preference for balance.
mash_bill: ~76% corn, ~20% wheat, ~4% malted barley (BT wheated mash bill, undisclosed)
why: CYBP stands for Craft Your Own Private Batch , a bottling program where participants blend their own expressions from three Weller barrels. The wheated mash bill and high proof make it one of the most coveted Weller releases.

name: Antique 107
tableside: Weller Antique 107 is 10 years old and 107 proof — the full-strength Weller experience. Same wheated mashbill, more age, more proof. For a guest who's worked through Special Reserve and wants to go further, this is the move.
story: Weller Antique 107 occupies a distinctive place in the Weller lineup: 10 years of aging and 107 proof, which sits between the approachability of Special Reserve and the intensity of the Weller Full Proof. The extra proof and extended aging push the wheated character into more serious territory , bolder caramel, more oak, a warmth that the lower-proof expressions don't have. For guests who know the Weller line, it's the natural next step, and for those new to wheated bourbon it's an excellent showcase of what age and proof do to this style.
trivia: 107 proof (53.5% ABV) is a traditional bourbon proof point with roots in the nineteenth century, when higher-proof spirits were common before the industry standardized around 80 proof.
Weller Antique 107 was one of the first Weller expressions to gain significant collector attention, predating the current wheated bourbon craze by nearly a decade.
At 10 years old, Weller Antique 107 has spent more time in the barrel than many of its allocated counterparts at higher price points.
mash_bill: ~76% corn, ~20% wheat, ~4% malted barley (BT wheated mash bill, undisclosed)
why: Weller Antique 107 is bottled at 107 proof , higher than the other Weller expressions , which amplifies the sweetness of the wheated mash bill. Considered by many to be the best value in the Weller lineup.

name: Master's Keep Voyage
tableside: Wild Turkey's Master's Keep series is their prestige line, and Voyage finishes in Appleton Estate rum casks from Jamaica. Tropical fruit, rum sweetness, and Wild Turkey's characteristic bold oak — an unusual combination that genuinely works.
story: Wild Turkey Master's Keep is master distiller Eddie Russell's platform for limited annual releases that push beyond the standard Wild Turkey lineup. Eddie is the son of Jimmy Russell, who spent over 60 years at the distillery and is one of the longest-serving active master distillers in bourbon history. Voyage is the rum-finished expression, aged in Appleton Estate Jamaica rum casks, a collaboration that brings Caribbean sweetness and tropical fruit notes to Wild Turkey's characteristically bold Kentucky bourbon base. The combination works because Wild Turkey's high rye content and barrel entry proof give the spirit enough structure to hold the rum cask influence without being overwhelmed.
trivia: Eddie Russell is the son of Jimmy Russell, who has worked at Wild Turkey for over 60 years — making the Russells the longest-serving father-son team in bourbon history.
Appleton Estate is Jamaica's oldest sugar estate and distillery, producing rum since 1749 — the casks carry centuries of Caribbean spirit tradition into Kentucky bourbon.
Wild Turkey uses a higher char level on their barrels than most Kentucky distilleries, which contributes to the brand's distinctively bold, tannic character that stands up to exotic cask finishes.
mash_bill: 75% corn, 13% rye, 12% malted barley
why: Master's Keep Voyage is finished in Spanish Oloroso sherry casks. Wild Turkey rarely does cask finishes, making this a notable departure from their standard lineup.

tableside: Woodford Reserve is the reliable standard — smooth, well-balanced, consistently good at $13. It's the bourbon equivalent of a well-made house wine: never wrong, always appropriate.
story: Woodford Reserve is produced at the historic Labrot & Graham Distillery in Versailles, Kentucky , a National Historic Landmark that is one of the most picturesque distilleries in the state. It was the first small-batch bourbon produced by Brown-Forman and helped define the premium bourbon category in the 1990s. Master distiller Chris Morris uses a triple distillation process in copper pot stills, unusual for Kentucky bourbon, which produces the smooth, rounded character the brand is known for. It's now the official bourbon of the Kentucky Derby.
trivia: Woodford Reserve is the official bourbon of the Kentucky Derby, served at Churchill Downs since 1999 — including in the iconic mint julep that is the race's signature drink.
The Woodford Reserve Distillery uses copper pot stills for triple distillation, a method more common in Scotch whisky production than Kentucky bourbon.
The distillery site in Versailles has been producing whiskey since 1812, making it one of the oldest continuously operated distillery sites in the United States.
mash_bill: 72% corn, 18% rye, 10% malted barley
why: Brown-Forman's flagship small batch bourbon, distilled at the historic Woodford Reserve Distillery in Versailles, Kentucky. One of the only major bourbons made using a combination of pot still and column still distillation.

name: Batch Proof
tableside: Woodford Reserve at full barrel strength — no dilution, no chill filtration. At 62.35% it has significant heat but the oak and caramel handle it well. A few drops of water open it up considerably if a guest wants to explore.
story: Woodford Reserve Batch Proof is released twice annually, in spring and fall, at the natural proof of the barrels selected that season. No water is added, which means each batch is a slightly different proof and a slightly different whiskey, reflecting the natural variation in how barrels develop across Woodford's aging warehouses. The standard Woodford is bottled at a fixed 45.2% by adding Kentucky limestone water to achieve consistency. Batch Proof removes that step, giving drinkers the most direct experience of what comes out of the barrel at the historic Labrot and Graham Distillery.
trivia: Batch Proof varies slightly in ABV from release to release because it reflects the actual barrel strength of each batch — there is no target proof, just what the barrels naturally produce.
At over 62% ABV, Woodford Batch Proof qualifies as 'cask strength' — a designation that means nothing has been added or removed since the barrel was emptied.
Adding a few drops of water to high-proof bourbon is a legitimate tasting technique that releases aromatic compounds and softens the alcohol burn, often revealing more flavor complexity.
mash_bill: 72% corn, 18% rye, 10% malted barley
why: Woodford Reserve's barrel proof expression, released twice annually in limited batches. Each batch is a slightly different proof depending on natural evaporation during aging.

name: 19th Master's Collection
detail: Kentucky · 45.2% · Sonoma Brandy & Pinot Noir Finish
tableside: Finished in Sonoma brandy and Pinot Noir barrels — California wine country meets Kentucky bourbon. Red berry, lighter tannins, and the Woodford base underneath. A great recommendation for a wine drinker who's bourbon-curious.
story: Woodford Reserve's Master's Collection is an annual limited release series exploring unusual production techniques or finishing approaches. The 19th edition uses barrels from Sonoma County, California , specifically brandy barrels and Pinot Noir wine barrels , to add a California wine country dimension to the Kentucky bourbon base. The combination of brandy barrel richness and Pinot Noir's lighter red fruit produces a distinctly different Woodford that appeals to guests who wouldn't normally gravitate toward bourbon.
trivia: Sonoma County produces some of California's most acclaimed Pinot Noir, particularly from the Russian River Valley appellation — the wine residue in finished barrels carries regional character.
Woodford Reserve has released a Master's Collection expression every year since 2005, each one exploring a different aspect of bourbon production or finishing.
Pinot Noir cask finishing is unusual in bourbon because the grape produces relatively delicate wine — the cask influence is subtler than Cabernet or Merlot, adding nuance rather than boldness.
mash_bill: 72% corn, 18% rye, 10% malted barley
why: The 19th annual Master's Collection release. Woodford releases a different limited expression each year exploring unusual grains, finishes, or production techniques.

name: 20th Master's Collection
tableside: Madeira is a fortified Portuguese wine known for intense dried fruit and nutty complexity — finishing bourbon in those barrels adds a layer of European richness that's immediately noticeable. The 20th Master's Collection is a milestone release worth marking.
story: The 20th Master's Collection is a milestone release for Woodford Reserve's annual limited series, and it uses Madeira casks to mark the occasion. Madeira is a fortified wine from the Portuguese island of the same name, famous for its extraordinary longevity , some Madeiras are drinkable for over a century. The oxidative aging process that defines Madeira production produces dried fruit, nut, and caramel complexity that translates beautifully into bourbon finishing, adding European sophistication to Kentucky tradition.
trivia: Madeira wine is one of the world's most durable wines — it was a favorite of the American Founding Fathers, and bottles from the 1700s are still drinkable today.
Madeira's unique flavor comes partly from intentional oxidation and heat exposure during production, called 'estufagem,' which caramelizes sugars and creates complex dried fruit notes.
The 20th Master's Collection marks two decades of the Woodford Reserve annual limited release program — one of the longest-running whiskey innovation series in American bourbon.
mash_bill: 72% corn, 18% rye, 10% malted barley
why: The 20th annual Master's Collection , a milestone release using a unique production method chosen by master distiller Chris Morris to mark two decades of limited releases.

name: 21st Master's Collection
tableside: Chinkapin oak produces more wood sugar than standard white oak — finishing in it adds a distinctive sweetness and a different tannin structure. At 55.2% this has real intensity. The most wood-forward Woodford on the list.
story: The 21st Master's Collection explores Chinkapin oak , a native American oak species (Quercus muehlenbergii) that produces higher concentrations of certain wood sugars than the standard American white oak (Quercus alba) used for most bourbon barrels. The result is a noticeably sweeter, more intensely wood-forward bourbon that challenges assumptions about what barrel choice can do. Bottled at full strength without dilution, it's both the most intense Woodford on the list and the most technically interesting from a cooperage perspective.
trivia: Chinkapin oak grows throughout the eastern United States and was historically used for furniture and flooring — its use in spirits production is relatively recent and experimental.
Different oak species contain varying levels of vanillin, lactones, and tannins — the specific compounds that give whiskey its wood-derived flavors of vanilla, coconut, and astringency.
Buffalo Trace also experiments with Chinkapin oak in their Old Charter Oak series, making it possible to compare how different distilleries interpret the same unusual wood species.
mash_bill: 72% corn, 18% rye, 10% malted barley
why: The 21st annual Master's Collection. Each year's release is a genuinely different whiskey, not just a different age statement of the same recipe.

name: Bottled in Bond
detail: Kentucky · 50% · Sazerac BBL · Single Barrel · VA Select
tableside: 1792 Bottled in Bond is a Virginia ABC select barrel — meaning the state hand-picked this single barrel from the distillery. At $16 it's the best value single barrel on the list. The BIB designation adds 100 proof credibility.
story: 1792 is produced by Barton 1792 Distillery in Bardstown, Kentucky , one of the largest and oldest distilleries in the state, named for the year Kentucky became a state. The Bottled-in-Bond expression carries the full weight of that federal designation: 100 proof, single distillery, single season, aged in a government-bonded warehouse. The Virginia ABC select barrel program adds another layer of curation , state buyers tasting through available barrels and selecting specific ones for Virginia accounts. The result is a single barrel with regional provenance at an entry-level price.
trivia: 1792 is named for the year Kentucky was admitted to the Union as the 15th state — an appropriate name for a distillery in Bardstown, the heart of bourbon country.
The Virginia ABC select barrel program sends buyers to distilleries to hand-select individual barrels, which are then bottled and distributed exclusively through Virginia ABC stores.
Barton 1792 Distillery is one of the few remaining distilleries in Bardstown to operate its own on-site cooperage, allowing precise control over barrel specifications.
mash_bill: 75% corn, 15% rye, 10% malted barley
why: 1792 is produced at Barton Distillery in Bardstown, one of Kentucky's oldest continuously operating distilleries. The Bottled in Bond expression is 100 proof, 100% Kentucky, and 4+ years old , the definition of classic American standards.

name: VA Select — Port Cask
detail: Kentucky · 55% · Port Cask · Single Barrel · VA Select
tableside: The single barrel version of Angel's Envy — same port cask finish, but from one specific barrel selected by Virginia buyers at cask strength. Noticeably more intense than the standard pour. A significant step up.
story: Angel's Envy VA Select is a Virginia-exclusive single barrel release, chosen by state buyers from Angel's Envy's port cask finishing inventory. Where the standard Angel's Envy is a blend of multiple barrels diluted to 43.3%, this is one barrel at full cask strength, the purest expression of what the port cask finish does to Lincoln Henderson's original recipe. Port wine casks contribute red fruit, tannin, and a sweetness that differs from grape variety to grape variety, meaning every single barrel selection produces a subtly different pour. Each bottle on the list reflects a specific moment in the distillery's aging program that cannot be exactly repeated.
trivia: Single barrel bourbon means every drop in the bottle — and every pour from that bottle — came from one specific oak barrel, identified by its warehouse location and barrel number.
Port cask finishing adds anthocyanins from the wine residue, which contributes to the darker color and dried fruit character distinguishing single barrel Angel's Envy from the standard release.
Virginia's ABC select barrel program is one of the most active in the country, with buyers visiting dozens of distilleries annually to hand-select exclusive single barrels.
mash_bill: 72% corn, 18% rye, 10% malted barley
why: A Virginia-exclusive single barrel selection from Angel's Envy, finished in port wine casks. The port finish adds red fruit and a slight tannic grip that distinguishes it from the standard Angel's Envy lineup.

name: VA Select — Tawny Cask
detail: Kentucky · 55% · Tawny Cask · Single Barrel · VA Select
tableside: Tawny port barrels age longer than ruby port barrels, developing more oxidative, nutty character. This finish adds dried fig, toffee, and a nutty depth that the port cask version doesn't have — richer and more complex at $38.
story: The Tawny Cask VA Select is the counterpart to the Port Cask version, both chosen by Virginia buyers from the Angel's Envy single barrel program. Tawny port is aged longer and in smaller barrels than ruby port before bottling, undergoing an oxidative aging process that concentrates the wine and produces nutty, dried fig, and caramel complexity quite different from the fresher red fruit of ruby port. That extended oxidative character transfers into the bourbon during finishing, creating a noticeably richer and more layered result. For guests who love sherry-influenced Scotch, the tawny cask version is the stronger recommendation.
trivia: Tawny port is aged in small oak barrels for 10 to over 40 years, during which oxidation changes its color from ruby red to amber-brown — the 'tawny' that names the style.
The oxidative aging that defines tawny port produces rancio character — a complex, nutty, aged quality found in long-aged wines and spirits that is highly prized by connoisseurs.
At $38, the Tawny Cask sits $8 above the Port Cask VA Select — a premium that reflects both the more complex cask and the rarity of tawny port barrels available for finishing.
mash_bill: 72% corn, 18% rye, 10% malted barley
why: Same Angel's Envy Virginia single barrel program but finished in tawny port casks instead of ruby port. The tawny finish adds oxidative, nutty notes rather than the fresh red fruit of the ruby port barrels.

name: 9 Year Sour Mash
tableside: Belle Meade's sour mash single barrel at cask strength — the sour mash process uses spent mash from a previous distillation to start the next fermentation, adding consistency and a subtle tangy complexity. Nine years old and full proof.
story: The sour mash process is actually standard practice for most American whiskey , distilleries add spent mash from a previous batch to the new fermentation to control pH and ensure consistency across batches. Belle Meade's Sour Mash expression highlights this process as a feature rather than a background technical detail, celebrating the traditional Tennessee approach to fermentation. At nearly 55% and nine years old, it's the most intense expression in the Belle Meade lineup and a limited release that reflects the distillery's growing confidence in its aging program.
trivia: The sour mash process was standardized in the American whiskey industry by Dr. James Crow in the early 1800s, and is now used by virtually every major bourbon and Tennessee whiskey producer.
The 'sour' in sour mash refers to the pH-lowering effect of the spent mash, which inhibits bacterial growth during fermentation and promotes consistent yeast activity.
Belle Meade's limited release program has grown significantly in recent years as Nelson's Green Brier Distillery builds its own aged inventory to complement sourced whiskey.
mash_bill: 64% corn, 30% rye, 6% malted barley
why: Belle Meade's 9-year sour mash expression, sourced from MGP's high-rye mash bill. The sour mash process , using a portion of the previous batch to start fermentation , produces a more consistent, slightly tangy character.

tableside: Blanton's was the world's first commercially marketed single barrel bourbon, released in 1984. The distinctive bottle with the horse and jockey stopper is iconic. Each bottle is hand-labeled with the barrel number, rick house, and dump date.
story: Blanton's Original Single Barrel is a landmark in American whiskey history , when it launched in 1984, the concept of bottling from a single barrel and celebrating that provenance on the label was revolutionary. Before Blanton's, bourbon was a commodity blended for consistency. Master distiller Elmer T. Lee, who created the expression, chose barrels from Warehouse H at Buffalo Trace , a metal-sided warehouse that experiences more dramatic temperature swings than traditional rick houses, accelerating aging and producing distinctive flavor. The horse and jockey stoppers come in eight collectible varieties, one for each letter of B-L-A-N-T-O-N-S.
trivia: Blanton's was created by Elmer T. Lee, a master distiller at Buffalo Trace who was inspired to bottle single barrels after being challenged to find the best barrel in the warehouse.
The eight collectible stopper designs — each a horse and jockey in a different racing pose — spell out B-L-A-N-T-O-N-S when arranged in sequence.
Blanton's was originally marketed almost exclusively in Japan, where single malt Scotch had created strong demand for premium, provenance-driven spirits before American consumers caught on.
mash_bill: ~87% corn, ~9% rye, ~4% malted barley (BT Mash Bill #2, undisclosed)
why: The original single barrel bourbon, first bottled in 1984 by Elmer T. Lee. Every bottle comes from a single barrel, hand-selected, and hand-dipped in red wax. The numbered stopper is a collector's piece in its own right.

name: Gold
tableside: Blanton's Gold is the higher-proof sibling of the Original — same single barrel approach, same Warehouse H selection, but bottled at 51.5% instead of 46.5%. The extra proof adds intensity and warmth while keeping the characteristic Blanton's profile.
story: Blanton's Gold was originally created for the export market, specifically Japan and Europe, before finding its way back to American shelves as global demand for the line grew. Japan has long been one of the most sophisticated whisky markets in the world, and the Gold was designed to meet those elevated expectations at a higher proof point. At 51.5% it sits meaningfully above the Original's 46.5%, giving it more intensity without reaching the full heat of Straight from the Barrel. For guests who love Blanton's Original and want to understand what proof does to the same base whiskey, the Gold is the most instructive next step.
trivia: Blanton's Gold was originally released exclusively for international markets in the late 1980s, reflecting the stronger global appetite for premium American whiskey before the domestic bourbon boom.
The difference between Blanton's Original and Gold is primarily proof — the same single barrel selection process and Warehouse H sourcing applies to both expressions.
Blanton's Gold is sometimes easier to find than the Original despite its higher price, as it attracts slightly less frenzied collector attention.
mash_bill: ~87% corn, ~9% rye, ~4% malted barley (BT Mash Bill #2, undisclosed)
why: Blanton's Gold is bottled at 103 proof rather than the standard 93 proof. The higher proof amplifies the flavor of the same BT Mash Bill 2 that produces the classic Blanton's.

name: Straight from the Barrel
tableside: Blanton's at full barrel strength — no water added, straight from the cask. At 67.2% it's serious heat, but the caramel and oak handle it. A few drops of water transform it. The most intense Blanton's expression and among the rarest.
story: Blanton's Straight from the Barrel is the purest expression of what Warehouse H single barrel selection produces , bottled at full cask strength without any dilution, water addition, or chill filtration. At around 67% ABV it's formidable, but the concentration of caramel, oak, and fruit compounds that develop at barrel strength reward careful sipping. Adding a few drops of water opens the aroma dramatically and softens the alcohol, making it accessible to guests who aren't accustomed to cask strength whiskey. It is also among the rarest Blanton's expressions to find.
trivia: Blanton's Straight from the Barrel was created primarily for the Japanese market, where cask strength expressions had a dedicated following before the practice became mainstream in America.
At cask strength, bourbon retains aromatic compounds — esters, lactones, and higher alcohols — that are partially lost when water is added for dilution, making high-proof expressions more aromatic.
Each bottle of Blanton's Straight from the Barrel displays the exact ABV of that specific barrel, which can vary by several percentage points from barrel to barrel.
mash_bill: ~87% corn, ~9% rye, ~4% malted barley (BT Mash Bill #2, undisclosed)
why: Straight from the Barrel is Blanton's at full cask strength , uncut and unfiltered. The proof varies by barrel, which means every bottle is genuinely different from every other bottle.

name: Pinkie's Batch
tableside: Booker's is always uncut, unfiltered, and straight from the barrel — full proof, no compromise. 'Pinkie's Batch' is named in honor of someone in the Beam family. At 61.2% it's intense but beautifully balanced for the proof level.
story: Booker's Bourbon was created by Booker Noe , Jim Beam's grandson and master distiller , who wanted to share bourbon the way distillers actually drink it: straight from the barrel, uncut and unfiltered. Each batch is named in honor of people and places meaningful to the Beam family legacy. 'Pinkie's Batch' continues that tradition of personal tribute while delivering the signature Booker's experience: full proof, full flavor, no compromise. It's designed to be enjoyed with a small splash of water, which the distillery says opens the nose and softens the heat.
trivia: Booker Noe began sharing barrel-proof bourbon with friends and family decades before Booker's was officially released in 1988 — the commercial product was essentially what he'd been drinking privately for years.
Each Booker's batch name is chosen personally by Fred Noe, Booker's son and current master distiller, to honor people and moments significant to the Beam family.
Booker's recommends adding a small amount of water to their bourbon — an unusual suggestion from a distillery, but one that genuinely improves the tasting experience at these proof levels.
mash_bill: 75% corn, 13% rye, 12% malted barley
why: Named after Booker Noe's late wife. Pinkie's Batch is an annual limited release in the Booker's lineup, which consistently produces some of the most intense uncut, unfiltered Jim Beam whiskey available.

name: Little Book 2025
detail: Kentucky · 60.9% · Chapter 9 'None For Granted' · Barrel Proof
tableside: Little Book is Freddie Noe's personal project — Booker's grandson blending different whiskeys to create something beyond what any single barrel can offer. Chapter 9 is called 'None For Granted' and reflects his philosophy of taking nothing in the craft for granted.
story: Little Book is the personal creative project of Freddie Noe, the eighth generation of the Beam family in American whiskey and son of current master distiller Fred Noe. Where the mainline Jim Beam products follow established recipes, Little Book is where Freddie experiments, blending different ages, mash bills, and grain types to create whiskeys that wouldn't fit anywhere else in the portfolio. Each annual chapter reflects his own palate and philosophy. The 2025 release, None For Granted, is a tribute to not taking the craft, the tradition, or the opportunity for granted, a message that carries weight from someone who grew up surrounded by bourbon history.
trivia: Freddie Noe represents the eighth generation of the Beam family in whiskey — a lineage stretching back to Johannes Jacob Böhm, who emigrated from Germany and began distilling in Kentucky in the late 1700s.
Little Book chapters are released annually and often sell out within days, driven by Freddie Noe's growing reputation as an innovative blender in his own right.
