Chapter 9's name 'None For Granted' reflects a philosophy Freddie has spoken about publicly — that in a family with such deep whiskey heritage, the pressure to honor that legacy requires constant humility.
mash_bill: 75% corn, 13% rye, 12% malted barley
why: Little Book is Booker Noe's grandson Freddie Noe's blending project , combining different whiskeys from across the Jim Beam portfolio each year. The 2025 expression is a new composition annually.

tableside: Bulleit at full barrel strength — the high-rye spice character that defines the brand becomes significantly more intense at 60.9%. This is for the guest who loves Bulleit's dryness and spice and wants to experience it without compromise.
story: Bulleit Barrel Proof takes the high-rye mash bill that defines the standard Bulleit, 68% corn and 28% rye, and bottles it straight from the barrel without dilution. The rye spice that gives standard Bulleit its dry character becomes markedly more pronounced at full proof. Bulleit sources its whiskey from Four Roses Distillery in Lawrenceburg under a supply agreement, meaning this is Four Roses distillate at its most transparent. At its price point it is one of the most accessible barrel proof expressions from a named Kentucky source on any serious list.
trivia: Bulleit's 28% rye content in the mashbill is nearly double the rye percentage of most standard Kentucky bourbons, which typically use 10–15% rye.
Barrel proof bourbon has become significantly more popular in recent years as consumers have grown more comfortable with high-proof spirits and more interested in unfiltered, uncompromised whiskey.
At 60.9% ABV, Bulleit Barrel Proof is among the highest-proof offerings from any major bourbon brand — a significant departure from the 45% of the standard expression.
mash_bill: 68% corn, 28% rye, 4% malted barley
why: Bulleit's single barrel expression, selected from their high-rye mash bill barrels. The 28% rye content produces a notably spice-forward character that stands up to neat sipping at barrel proof.

name: 5 Year
tableside: Clyde May's is an Alabama whiskey with deep roots in the state's moonshine tradition — the brand honors a legendary bootlegger. The 5 Year VA Select is a Virginia-picked single barrel at a fair price for the quality inside.
story: Clyde May's is named after a legendary Alabama moonshiner and World War II veteran who became one of the most celebrated illicit distillers in state history. His whiskey was known for its quality, hand-built stills, and distinctive apple-scented barrels. The modern brand, legalized and revived by his son Kenny May in 2001, was designated the official state spirit of Alabama by legislative resolution in 2004. The single barrel VA Select releases strip away the apple finish to show the base whiskey character at full proof, an honest expression of a brand with genuinely unusual American roots.
trivia: Clyde May was designated Alabama's 'Official State Spirit' in 2004 by the state legislature — an unusual honor for a man who spent years evading federal revenue agents.

The historical Clyde May was known for adding dried apples to his moonshine barrels, a practice that gave the whiskey a distinctive fruity character that the modern brand references in its standard expression.
Clyde May's is now produced in Kentucky while maintaining its Alabama identity and story — a common arrangement for craft spirits brands with regional heritage but national production needs.
mash_bill: Undisclosed — sourced and aged by Clyde May's
why: Clyde May's started as Alabama's official state spirit , the brand traces its roots to Clyde May, a legendary Alabama moonshiner. The 5 Year is one of the most unusual bourbons on the list.

name: 6 Year
tableside: One more year in the barrel than the 5 Year — noticeably more oak presence and a slightly deeper character at the same price. Worth doing a side by side if a guest is curious about how a single year of aging changes the spirit.
story: The Clyde May's 6 Year VA Select sits alongside the 5 Year as a direct comparison piece, same distillery, same single barrel selection program, one additional year of Kentucky aging. The extra year adds noticeable tannin structure and deepens the caramel character without fundamentally changing the approachable Clyde May's profile. For staff, having both expressions available simultaneously creates a natural opportunity to walk a guest through what one year of barrel aging actually produces in a bourbon's flavor development. It's a side-by-side that sells itself.
trivia: A single year of Kentucky bourbon aging can add significant color, tannin, and flavor complexity due to the state's extreme temperature swings — barrels absorb and release whiskey with each seasonal cycle.
Virginia ABC select barrel programs often choose multiple expressions from the same distillery to allow direct comparison, as with these two Clyde May's single barrels.
The 0.9% ABV difference between the 5 Year and 6 Year reflects natural barrel variation — each barrel reaches its own proof level through the aging process without human intervention.
mash_bill: Undisclosed — sourced and aged by Clyde May's
why: An additional year of aging beyond the 5 Year, allowing the wood influence to deepen. Clyde May's uses a proprietary mash bill and production process that sets it apart from Kentucky bourbons.

detail: Kentucky · 50% · Bottled in Bond · Single Barrel
tableside: E.H. Taylor Single Barrel is Bottled in Bond from one specific barrel — the same federal quality standard as the Small Batch, but the added provenance of a single cask. Every bottle from this barrel tells exactly the same story from exactly the same source.
story: E.H. Taylor Single Barrel combines two of bourbon's most meaningful designations: Bottled-in-Bond and single barrel. The BIB requirements, 100 proof, one distillery, one distilling season, and government-bonded warehouse aging, were standards Colonel Taylor himself championed when he lobbied for the Bottled-in-Bond Act of 1897. The single barrel designation adds complete provenance: every drop came from one specific oak container in one specific warehouse location at Buffalo Trace. Together they represent the highest level of transparency in American whiskey, from a man who spent his life fighting for exactly that.
trivia: E.H. Taylor Single Barrel bottles are labeled with the specific barrel number and warehouse location, allowing enthusiasts to track and compare different barrels from the same expression.
The combination of BIB and single barrel is relatively rare — most BIB bourbons are batched from multiple barrels to ensure the consistent flavor profile required by the designation.
Buffalo Trace selects E.H. Taylor Single Barrel releases from its oldest and most well-positioned warehouses, where temperature variation and aging conditions are most favorable.
mash_bill: ~75% corn, ~13% rye, ~12% malted barley (BT Mash Bill #1, undisclosed)
why: E.H. Taylor is Colonel Edmund Haynes Taylor Jr., the man who built Buffalo Trace Distillery and championed the Bottled-in-Bond Act of 1897. Single Barrel is a direct expression of his legacy.

name: Barrel Proof
tableside: E.H. Taylor Barrel Proof is one of the most sought-after releases in the Buffalo Trace lineup — full proof, unfiltered, and carrying the full weight of the Taylor name. At 65.5% it demands respect but rewards patience.
story: E.H. Taylor Barrel Proof is among the most allocated expressions in the Buffalo Trace portfolio, which is significant given how many sought-after releases the distillery manages annually. Released in limited quantities without dilution or chill filtration, it's the most intense Taylor expression available, showing what Buffalo Trace's Mash Bill 1 becomes when given full aging time and bottled without compromise. The Colonel Taylor Barrel Proof releases are numbered and traceable, maintaining the transparency and accountability standards that Colonel Taylor championed over a century ago.
trivia: E.H. Taylor Barrel Proof is one of the few expressions in the Buffalo Trace Antique Collection released outside of the annual fall window, making it available — when it appears — at unexpected moments.

Chill filtration, which removes oils and compounds that can cause cloudiness at low temperatures, is deliberately omitted from barrel proof releases — preserving full flavor at the cost of cosmetic clarity.
At 65.5%, E.H. Taylor Barrel Proof is among the highest-proof expressions in the Buffalo Trace lineup, second only to the George T. Stagg in some years.
mash_bill: ~75% corn, ~13% rye, ~12% malted barley (BT Mash Bill #1, undisclosed)
why: E.H. Taylor Barrel Proof is released in limited quantities and never diluted. One of the most anticipated annual releases from Buffalo Trace, often allocated before it reaches most markets.

name: VA Select
detail: Kentucky · 62.15% · Single Barrel · Barrel Proof · VA Select
tableside: Elijah Craig barrel proof VA Select — Virginia buyers chose this specific barrel from Heaven Hill's inventory. At 62.15% and $28, it's one of the best value barrel proof pours on the list. Elijah Craig consistently punches above its price.
story: Elijah Craig is named after a Baptist minister and Kentucky pioneer credited by some historians with being among the first to age corn whiskey in charred oak barrels , a practice that defines bourbon today. Whether or not the legend is historically accurate, the Heaven Hill expression that carries his name has built a well-deserved reputation for quality at an honest price. The VA Select barrel proof release is a Virginia-exclusive, chosen by state buyers from Heaven Hill's barrel proof inventory , the best available value in high-proof bourbon on this list.
trivia: Elijah Craig the minister is often credited with 'inventing' bourbon by aging corn whiskey in charred oak — a claim that bourbon historians debate, as the charring practice likely developed independently across multiple distilleries.
Heaven Hill's Elijah Craig line has won more awards at international spirits competitions than almost any other American whiskey at its price point.
The VA Select designation means Virginia ABC buyers tasted through available barrels and chose this one specifically for Virginia distribution — a genuine curation process.
mash_bill: 78% corn, 10% rye, 12% malted barley
why: A Virginia-exclusive single barrel selection from Elijah Craig, hand-picked for the state's market. Heaven Hill's standard mash bill aged longer and bottled at single barrel proof.

name: 18 Year
tableside: Eighteen years in Kentucky oak is a long time — the wood dominates but doesn't overwhelm, and there's a leather and dried fruit complexity that only comes with serious age. One of the oldest single barrels on the list at a price that's still reasonable.
story: Elijah Craig 18 Year is a rare age statement for Kentucky bourbon , most producers pull barrels well before 18 years to avoid over-oaking, but Heaven Hill's deep inventory and careful warehouse management allows them to identify barrels that have aged gracefully this long. The result is a bourbon where oak is the dominant force, but a force balanced by decades of caramelization and fruit development rather than raw tannin. Each barrel is unique, and the single barrel designation means every bottle is a specific conversation with one particular piece of wood.
trivia: Kentucky's climate means bourbon ages significantly faster than Scotch whisky — 18 years in Kentucky is often compared to 30+ years in Scotland in terms of wood extraction.
Heaven Hill's ability to release 18-year bourbon at a relatively accessible price reflects the distillery's enormous aging inventory, which includes barrels laid down across multiple decades.

Elijah Craig 18 Year was originally released as part of the standard lineup before transitioning to a limited single barrel release — a shift that reflects both rising demand and the challenges of maintaining an 18-year supply.
mash_bill: 78% corn, 10% rye, 12% malted barley
why: Elijah Craig 18 Year is one of the oldest regularly available Heaven Hill expressions. The extended aging produces a pronounced oak character that defines the upper range of the Heaven Hill lineup.

tableside: Elmer T. Lee was the master distiller who created Blanton's — the world's first single barrel bourbon. This expression bearing his name is a tribute from Buffalo Trace to his legacy. Approachable, elegant, and historically significant.
story: Elmer T. Lee spent over 40 years at Buffalo Trace Distillery, eventually retiring as master distiller in 1985, but not before creating Blanton's Original Single Barrel in 1984, which launched the entire single barrel category in American whiskey. Without Elmer T. Lee, there would be no Blanton's, no Buffalo Trace Antique Collection, and arguably no modern premium bourbon movement. The expression bearing his name was released after his passing in 2013 at age 93. It uses the same Warehouse H single barrel selection philosophy he pioneered, bottled at an approachable proof that reflects his preference for balance over intensity.
trivia: Elmer T. Lee's creation of Blanton's in 1984 is credited with launching the premium bourbon segment in America — before Blanton's, bourbon was largely considered a commodity spirit.
Lee worked at Buffalo Trace for over four decades, overseeing production through multiple ownership changes and the broader decline of American whiskey in the 1970s and 80s.
The Elmer T. Lee expression is released in very limited quantities and has become increasingly allocated since his passing, driven by collector demand and genuine appreciation for his legacy.
mash_bill: ~87% corn, ~9% rye, ~4% malted barley (BT Mash Bill #2, undisclosed)
why: Named after the master distiller who created Blanton's Single Barrel. Elmer T. Lee is produced in extremely limited quantities from Buffalo Trace's Mash Bill 2, and is one of the most elusive bourbons on this list.

name: 9 Year 9 Month
detail: Kentucky · 59.4% · Single Barrel · Barrel Proof · VA Select
tableside: Four Roses is unique in Kentucky bourbon — they use two mashbills and five yeast strains, creating ten distinct recipes they blend or bottle individually. This VA Select single barrel shows one specific recipe at full proof, chosen by Virginia buyers.
story: Four Roses' production system is unlike any other Kentucky distillery: two mashbills (one high-rye, one low-rye) combined with five proprietary yeast strains produce ten distinct bourbon recipes, each identified by a letter code on the bottle. Single barrel expressions bottle one of these ten recipes from one specific barrel, making them the most transparent and traceable bourbons in Kentucky production. The VA Select 9 Year 9 Month was chosen by Virginia buyers from Four Roses' barrel proof inventory , a specific recipe, a specific barrel, a specific moment in time.
trivia: Four Roses uses five yeast strains identified by the letters V, K, O, Q, and F — each produces distinctly different flavor compounds during fermentation, from floral and delicate to spicy and robust.
The two Four Roses mashbills are OBSO (low rye, 60% corn) and OBSK (high rye, 75% corn) — combined with five yeasts, these produce the ten recipes that define the distillery's output.
Four Roses was sold to Seagram's in the 1940s and its bourbon was redirected entirely to export markets for decades — American consumers couldn't buy Four Roses bourbon domestically until 2004.
mash_bill: 60% corn, 35% rye, 5% malted barley (Four Roses B mash bill)
why: Four Roses' OBSV recipe , their B mash bill (60% corn, 35% rye) with the V yeast strain, which adds floral and fruity notes. Selected at 9 years and 9 months old for an unusually specific age statement.

name: Limited Edition 2024
tableside: Four Roses annual Limited Edition Small Batch is their prestige release — master distiller Brent Elliott blends specific recipes from specific barrels to create something more complex than any single barrel can offer. The 2024 is the current vintage.
story: Four Roses Limited Edition Small Batch is released once annually and represents master distiller Brent Elliott's most personal creative statement. Four Roses is uniquely positioned for this kind of blending, because they produce ten distinct recipes, the combination of two mash bills and five yeast strains, giving Elliott more compositional options than any other American distillery. Rather than releasing a single recipe at a single age, he hand-selects specific barrels from specific recipes and blends them to a complexity no individual component could produce alone. The blend changes every year, making each vintage genuinely unique.
trivia: Four Roses Limited Edition is released in September each year with a small ceremony at the distillery — bottles allocated to accounts often sell out within hours of arrival.
Master distiller Brent Elliott tastes hundreds of individual barrel samples before selecting the specific recipe-barrel combinations that make the final Limited Edition blend.
The annual Limited Edition is considered one of the benchmark releases in American whiskey, consistently earning high scores from critics and strong secondary market interest from collectors.
mash_bill: Blend of Four Roses B and E mash bills
why: The annual Four Roses Limited Edition Small Batch blends multiple mash bills and yeast strains in a combination that changes every year. The 2024 release is a genuinely unique whiskey.

name: Limited Edition 2025
tableside: The 2025 vintage of Four Roses Limited Edition — same philosophy as the 2024, different barrels, different blend, different result. Having both on the list is a rare opportunity to taste how the same master distiller interprets different raw material across consecutive years.
story: Four Roses Limited Edition 2025 is the newest vintage of the annual prestige release, blended by master distiller Brent Elliott from a fresh selection of barrels and recipes from Four Roses' unique portfolio of ten distinct whiskey styles. Having both the 2024 and 2025 expressions on the list simultaneously is an unusual opportunity: it allows direct comparison of how the same blending philosophy and the same distillery produces different results from different source material and different harvest years. For guests who approach whiskey the way serious wine drinkers approach vintages, this is an exceptional educational experience.
trivia: The 0.4% ABV difference between the 2024 and 2025 Limited Editions reflects the different barrels selected — proof is determined by the natural barrel strength of the chosen components.
Four Roses deliberately varies the recipe composition of each Limited Edition, meaning the 2025 may use entirely different yeast-mashbill combinations than the 2024.

Comparing consecutive vintages of the same expression is a practice more common in wine culture than spirits — Four Roses' consistent annual Limited Edition makes it one of the few American whiskies where vintage comparison is genuinely meaningful.
mash_bill: Blend of Four Roses B and E mash bills
why: The 2025 Limited Edition uses Four Roses' highest-rye B mash bill combined with their lower-rye E mash bill in a blend that will not be reproduced. Once it is gone, it is gone.

name: 10 Year
detail: Kentucky · 50% · Bottled in Bond · Single Barrel
tableside: Henry McKenna 10 Year BIB Single Barrel won Best in Show at the San Francisco World Spirits Competition in 2019 — which caused immediate allocation problems. It's a 100 proof, ten year, single barrel bourbon at $30. The value is hard to argue with.
story: Henry McKenna 10 Year Bottled in Bond Single Barrel was a relatively quiet release until it won Best in Show at the 2019 San Francisco World Spirits Competition , one of the most prestigious blind tasting competitions in the industry. The win caused immediate and dramatic increases in demand, turning what had been an underappreciated value pour into a highly allocated bottle virtually overnight. The liquid itself hadn't changed: 100 proof, ten years old, single barrel, Heaven Hill production. The award simply focused attention on what attentive drinkers had known quietly for years.
trivia: The 2019 SFWSC Best in Show win for Henry McKenna is one of the most dramatic examples of a spirits award immediately and permanently changing a product's market status.
Henry McKenna was an Irish immigrant who established a distillery in Fairfield, Kentucky in 1855 — his brand was acquired by Heaven Hill in 1941.
At $30 for a BIB single barrel with a 10-year age statement, Henry McKenna remains one of the objectively best value propositions in allocated American whiskey.
mash_bill: 78% corn, 10% rye, 12% malted barley
why: Henry McKenna 10 Year is one of the few bottled-in-bond single barrel bourbons at its price point. 100 proof, single barrel, 10 years old , and consistently one of the best values in American whiskey.

tableside: John J. Bowman is a Virginia bourbon — distilled at Buffalo Trace in Kentucky, then shipped to A. Smith Bowman Distillery in Fredericksburg for a second distillation and aging. The Virginia connection is real and the price is honest.
story: John J. Bowman Single Barrel has a genuinely interesting production story: the distillate begins at Buffalo Trace in Kentucky, is shipped to A. Smith Bowman Distillery in Fredericksburg, Virginia, where it undergoes a second distillation before being barreled and aged in Virginia. This double distillation process produces a lighter, more delicate spirit than straight Kentucky bourbon, and the Virginia aging climate , slightly cooler and more humid than Kentucky , develops the wood influence differently. At $19 for a single barrel bourbon with this provenance, it represents exceptional value.
trivia: A. Smith Bowman Distillery in Fredericksburg, Virginia is one of the oldest legal distilleries in the Mid-Atlantic region, with roots going back to a farm distillery founded by Abram Smith Bowman after Prohibition.

The double distillation process used for John J. Bowman — once at Buffalo Trace, once at A. Smith Bowman — is unusual in American whiskey production and produces a notably cleaner, lighter distillate.
Virginia's distilling history predates Kentucky's — George Washington operated one of the largest whiskey distilleries in the young nation at Mount Vernon in the 1790s.
mash_bill: Blend of BT Mash Bill #1 and #2, triple distilled at A. Smith Bowman
why: Produced in Virginia at the A. Smith Bowman Distillery using a base distillate from Buffalo Trace that is then triple-distilled and aged in Virginia. The extra distillation produces a notably smooth character.

name: Wood Finishing Series 2025
tableside: Maker's Mark Wood Finishing Series uses custom stave inserts during the final aging period to add specific wood character. The Keeper's Release is the barrel proof version — full strength, no dilution. The most intense Maker's on the list at a surprisingly fair price.
story: Maker's Mark Wood Finishing Series began as an experiment in applying secondary wood character without a full barrel change. Custom-crafted stave inserts made from different wood types are placed inside the aging barrel for a final maturation period, extracting flavor compounds from the new wood without the dramatic impact of a full barrel swap. The Keeper's Release is the barrel proof version of the series, bottled without dilution at full cask strength. It gives guests the most direct possible experience of this wood-forward approach from one of Kentucky's most historic and recognizable distilleries.
trivia: The Wood Finishing Series stave inserts are made from American oak processed differently than standard barrel staves — air-dried, toasted, or charred to varying specifications to produce targeted flavor profiles.

Maker's Mark describes the Keeper's Release as the bottles set aside by distillery staff for their own enjoyment before the main allocation goes to market — a nod to the tradition of cellar masters keeping the best barrels.
The stave insert technique was developed by Maker's Mark master distiller Greg Davis as a way to add wood complexity without the risk of over-oaking that comes with a full secondary barrel finish.
mash_bill: 70% corn, 16% winter wheat, 14% malted barley
why: The Wood Finishing Series is Maker's Mark's platform for exploring different types of wood staves during finishing. The 2025 release uses a specific wood configuration chosen by the Maker's Mark sensory team.

name: 10 Year
tableside: Michter's 10 Year is one of the most decorated single barrel bourbons in production — decade of aging, carefully selected barrels, and Michter's climate-controlled warehouse philosophy. At $55 it earns its place at the top of the list.
story: Michter's 10 Year Single Barrel is released in very limited quantities and consistently earns critical acclaim that places it among the finest aged bourbons produced anywhere. Each barrel is individually selected after tasting by the production team, ensuring that only barrels that have developed real complexity make the cut. Michter's climate-controlled warehouses slow the aging process compared to traditional rick houses, producing more consistent development across different barrel positions , an approach that pays dividends particularly at the 10-year mark.
trivia: Michter's climate-controlled warehouses are designed to eliminate the dramatic temperature swings that characterize traditional rick house aging — a deliberate choice to slow and even out the extraction process.
Michter's 10 Year Single Barrel has won the title of 'World Whisky of the Year' from multiple major publications, placing it in the company of elite Scotch expressions.
Each bottle of Michter's 10 Year displays the barrel number it came from — a transparency that allows enthusiasts to identify and compare their experiences across different barrels.
mash_bill: Undisclosed — Kentucky rye bourbon, approximately 79% corn, 11% rye, 10% malted barley
why: Michter's 10 Year Rye is released in very limited quantities and has spent at least 11.5 years in barrel , master distiller Joe Magliocco has confirmed no barrel is released under that threshold.

name: 4 Grain
detail: Texas · 57% · Stave Spiral in Bottle · Single Barrel · VA Select
tableside: Oak and Eden puts a wood spiral inside the bottle — the whiskey continues to extract oak character after bottling. It sounds gimmicky but it's a real aging technique. The 4 Grain uses wheat, corn, rye, and malted barley for a complex base.
story: Oak and Eden is a Texas-based spirits company built around a patented innovation: a specially crafted wood spiral placed inside each bottle, allowing the whiskey to continue interacting with oak after bottling. The concept is an extension of traditional aging principles , wood contact produces flavor , applied at the retail level rather than exclusively at the distillery. The 4 Grain expression uses all four traditional American grain types in its mashbill, creating a complex base that interacts richly with the in-bottle spiral. The VA Select single barrel adds Virginia curation to the Texas production.
trivia: Oak and Eden holds a patent on their in-bottle wood spiral technology, which they call 'in-bottle finishing' — the wood continues to release compounds into the whiskey for the shelf life of the bottle.

The four grains in the 4 Grain expression — wheat, corn, rye, and malted barley — are the same four grains used in traditional American bourbon production, but in unusual proportions that distinguish it from standard mashbills.

In-bottle aging is a legitimate continuation of the same extraction chemistry that occurs in barrels — wood compounds including vanillin, lactones, and tannins migrate into the spirit through direct contact.
mash_bill: Four grain: corn, rye, wheat, malted barley (undisclosed percentages)
why: Oak and Eden finishes its whiskeys with a spiral of toasted wood inserted directly into the bottle , the spirit continues to interact with the wood after bottling. The 4 Grain expression uses corn, wheat, rye, and malted barley.

tableside: Russell's Reserve Single Barrel is Wild Turkey's premium expression, named for Jimmy and Eddie Russell. The VA Select means Virginia picked this barrel — at 58.6% it's serious proof with Wild Turkey's characteristic bold, spicy backbone.
story: Russell's Reserve is Wild Turkey's tribute to Jimmy Russell , who spent over six decades at the distillery, making him the longest-tenured master distiller in bourbon history , and his son Eddie, who continues his legacy. The single barrel expression allows the Russells' preferred style , bold, high-proof, with Wild Turkey's characteristic tannic backbone , to shine without the softening effect of blending. The VA Select designation means Virginia buyers went to the distillery, tasted through available barrels, and chose this specific one for the Virginia market.
trivia: Jimmy Russell began working at Wild Turkey in 1954 and has never worked anywhere else — his 70+ year tenure makes him the longest-serving active master distiller in the history of American whiskey.
Wild Turkey uses a higher char level on their barrels than most Kentucky distilleries and ages at a lower barrel entry proof, which contributes to the brand's distinctively bold, robust character.

Russell's Reserve was originally created as a 10-year expression specifically for Eddie Russell, who wanted a bourbon that reflected his own palate — richer and more structured than the standard Wild Turkey lineup.
mash_bill: 75% corn, 13% rye, 12% malted barley
why: Russell's Reserve is Wild Turkey's premium small batch expression, named after master distillers Jimmy and Eddie Russell. A higher-age, higher-quality version of Wild Turkey using the same 75/13/12 mash bill.

name: 13 Year
tableside: Thirteen years at Wild Turkey's bold aging style produces something formidable — deep oak, dark fruit, serious proof. The Spring 2025 release is the current vintage. One of the most intense expressions on the entire list.
story: Russell's Reserve 13 Year Barrel Proof is one of the most powerful expressions on the list. Thirteen years of Wild Turkey's bold aging approach, conducted in high-char barrels in Lawrenceburg's extreme Kentucky climate, produces something significantly more intense than comparable age statements from distilleries with gentler approaches. Eddie Russell selects each barrel personally, applying the same instincts his father Jimmy used for over six decades. The Spring 2025 release reflects the specific barrels available from that season's inspection, making it a genuine vintage expression that will not be repeated.
trivia: Wild Turkey's bold aging approach — high char barrels, lower warehouse positions, Kentucky climate exposure — means their 13-year bourbon has extracted more wood character than some distilleries' 20-year expressions.
Russell's Reserve Barrel Proof releases are designated by season — Spring and Fall — reflecting the distillery's practice of releasing limited expressions on a seasonal schedule.
At 61.8% ABV, Russell's Reserve 13 Year Barrel Proof requires careful sipping or a few drops of water to fully appreciate — the concentration of compounds at this proof level rewards patience.
mash_bill: 75% corn, 13% rye, 12% malted barley
why: A 13-year single barrel expression from Russell's Reserve , one of the oldest regular single barrel releases from Wild Turkey. Each barrel is individually selected by Eddie Russell.

name: Batch 25D
tableside: Stagg is the younger sibling of George T. Stagg — the most allocated bourbon in the Buffalo Trace Antique Collection. At 63.2% and $28, it's one of the most intense and best-value barrel proof bourbons on the list. This is not a casual pour.
story: Stagg (formerly known as Stagg Jr.) is named after George T. Stagg, the 19th century distillery owner whose name graces the most sought-after release in the Buffalo Trace Antique Collection. Where the original George T. Stagg is released once a year and nearly impossible to find, Stagg is released in numbered batches throughout the year, making it a more accessible entry point into the ultra-high-proof Buffalo Trace experience. Batch 25D designates the specific production batch , each batch varies slightly in proof and character based on the barrels selected.
trivia: George T. Stagg was a whiskey entrepreneur and distillery owner in the late 1800s who transformed what is now Buffalo Trace Distillery into one of the largest operations in Kentucky.
Stagg batch numbers follow a system where the first two digits indicate the year and the letter indicates the sequential batch within that year — 25D means the fourth batch released in 2025.
At over 63% ABV, Stagg is one of the highest-proof bourbons commercially available in the United States — only a handful of annual releases from Buffalo Trace reach higher proof levels.
mash_bill: ~75% corn, ~13% rye, ~12% malted barley (BT Mash Bill #1, undisclosed)
why: George T. Stagg is part of Buffalo Trace's annual Antique Collection , the most allocated, most anticipated American whiskey release of the year. Uncut, unfiltered, and released once annually in very limited quantities.

name: Full Proof
tableside: Weller Full Proof is the wheated bourbon at full barrel entry proof — 114 proof, same mashbill as Pappy, more intensity than Special Reserve or Antique 107. For a guest who loves Weller and wants to go further, this is the next conversation.
story: Weller Full Proof is bottled at the exact proof at which the distillate entered the barrel , 114 proof (57% ABV) , which is the legal maximum for bourbon entry proof plus the natural concentration that occurs during aging. The concept is that this proof point represents the most direct expression of the distillate's character as it began its oak interaction, before water addition changes the balance. Combined with the Van Winkle-shared wheated mashbill, it produces the most intense standard Weller expression and one of the most sought-after wheated bourbons at any price.
trivia: Bourbon law allows entry into the barrel at a maximum of 125 proof (62.5% ABV) — Weller Full Proof enters at 114 proof (57%), meaning some evaporation during aging has brought it to the same level as its bottling proof.

Full proof bottling — matching the bottling proof to the barrel entry proof — is a conceptually interesting approach that links the bottle's character directly to the distillation decisions made years earlier.
Weller Full Proof is often cited as the best entry point into high-proof wheated bourbon for guests already familiar with the standard Weller expressions.
mash_bill: ~76% corn, ~20% wheat, ~4% malted barley (BT wheated mash bill, undisclosed)
why: Weller Full Proof is bottled at 114 proof , the same proof at which it entered the barrel. No water is added, which means the whiskey you taste is as close to the barrel as possible without going fully cask strength.

name: Kentucky Spirit
tableside: Wild Turkey Kentucky Spirit is the distillery's flagship single barrel — Jimmy Russell's personal selection philosophy applied to one barrel at a time. The bold Wild Turkey character in its most transparent, provenance-driven form at a very fair price.
story: Wild Turkey Kentucky Spirit is the single barrel expression that carries Jimmy Russell's personal selection philosophy, the same approach he refined over six decades as master distiller at the Lawrenceburg, Kentucky distillery. Jimmy Russell is one of the longest-serving master distillers in bourbon history and is widely considered one of the great figures of the industry. Each bottle of Kentucky Spirit is labeled with barrel number, rick number, and floor number, providing complete provenance for every pour. The Wild Turkey house style, bold, tannic, and high-rye, is at its most transparent in a single barrel context with no blending to smooth the edges.
trivia: Wild Turkey Kentucky Spirit bottles display the complete barrel provenance: barrel number, rick house number, and floor level within the rick house — information that directly correlates to the barrel's aging conditions.
Jimmy Russell is known for personally tasting through potential Kentucky Spirit barrels, applying the same palate he has developed across seven decades of whiskey production.
Wild Turkey's rick houses age barrels on multiple floors, with upper floors experiencing more heat and producing bolder, more intense whiskey — lower floors age more slowly and gently.
mash_bill: 75% corn, 13% rye, 12% malted barley
why: Kentucky Spirit is Wild Turkey's single barrel expression. Each barrel is individually selected by master distiller Eddie Russell, who has been making Wild Turkey since 1964.

tableside: Angel's Envy Rye finishes in Caribbean rum casks — which adds tropical fruit and sweetness to the naturally spicy rye base. An unusual and genuinely successful combination. Great for a guest who loves the bourbon but wants something drier and more complex.
story: Angel's Envy Rye applies the same finishing philosophy that made the bourbon famous, unusual cask selection applied thoughtfully to a quality base spirit. Where the bourbon uses port casks to soften a sweet corn base, the rye uses Caribbean rum casks that previously held Cognac before their time in the Caribbean, adding tropical sweetness to a naturally spicy, dry grain spirit. The base is a 6 to 7 year old 95% rye mash bill from MGP in Indiana, one of the most intensely grain-forward rye recipes available. The contrast between the rye's inherent pepper and the rum cask's molasses-driven sweetness produces something genuinely surprising, a rye that rewards guests who think they don't like rye.
trivia: Angel's Envy Rye matures for up to 18 months in Caribbean rum casks after its initial aging — significantly longer than most cask finish programs, which typically run 3–6 months.
Caribbean rum casks carry residue from molasses-based rum production, contributing the distinctive tropical fruit and sugar cane sweetness that differentiates this rye from any other on the list.
Rye whiskey must contain at least 51% rye grain in the mashbill — the high rye content creates the spicy, dry backbone that makes cask finishing particularly dramatic by contrast.
mash_bill: 95% rye, 5% malted barley
why: A 95% rye mash bill from MGP , one of the spiciest rye bases available , finished for up to 18 months in Caribbean rum casks. The rum finish adds sweetness and tropical notes that soften the intense rye spice.

tableside: Bulleit Rye is 95% rye — one of the highest rye mashbills commercially available. Dry, spicy, and straightforward. The go-to recommendation for a guest who wants a classic rye character without complexity getting in the way.
story: Bulleit Rye uses a mash bill of 95% rye and 5% malted barley, sourced from MGP in Lawrenceburg, Indiana, the same facility that supplies a significant portion of the American craft whiskey industry. The high rye content pushes the grain's natural character, dry, peppery, and slightly herbal, to the foreground with very little sweetness to soften it. It is the most straightforward introduction to what rye whiskey actually tastes like, and at its price point it is the most accessible serious rye on the list. For a guest who has only had bourbon and wants to understand the difference, this is where to start.
trivia: Bulleit Rye's 95% rye mashbill is sourced from MGP Distillery in Lawrenceburg, Indiana — the same facility that produces high-rye mashbill rye for numerous American brands.
A 95% rye mashbill is near the practical maximum for rye whiskey production — higher rye content makes fermentation increasingly difficult due to the grain's sticky beta-glucan compounds.
Rye whiskey was America's original dominant whiskey style before Prohibition — bourbon's rise to dominance came afterward, when many rye distilleries in the Northeast and Mid-Atlantic never reopened.
mash_bill: 95% rye, 5% malted barley
why: Bulleit sources its rye from MGP in Indiana, where the 95% rye mash bill was developed. One of the most widely available and consistently excellent high-rye expressions on the market.

tableside: E.H. Taylor Rye carries the same Bottled-in-Bond credentials as the bourbon — 100 proof, single distillery, single season. The Taylor name applied to rye produces something more complex than the standard Buffalo Trace rye profile. Worth the allocated price.
story: E.H. Taylor Rye extends the Colonel's Bottled-in-Bond legacy into the rye category , applying the same federal quality standards that defined his bourbon career to a grain spirit he would have been familiar with from the pre-Prohibition era when rye dominated American whiskey. Buffalo Trace's rye production is less publicized than their bourbon program but equally serious, and the Taylor name signals a level of selection and care that goes beyond their standard rye releases. The BIB designation adds 100 proof authenticity to an already compelling bottle.
trivia: E.H. Taylor Jr. operated during an era when rye whiskey was America's most consumed spirit — his Bottled-in-Bond Act was written partly to protect the quality of rye as much as bourbon.
Buffalo Trace produces relatively small quantities of rye compared to their bourbon output, making E.H. Taylor Rye significantly harder to find than its bourbon counterpart.
The pre-Prohibition American whiskey landscape was dominated by rye from Maryland and Pennsylvania distilleries — bourbon's rise to prominence came after many of those operations never reopened.
mash_bill: 100% rye, 0% corn (rye and malted barley only, BT recipe)
why: The E.H. Taylor Rye uses a unique two-grain mash bill of rye and malted barley only , no corn. One of the only ryes from a major distillery to omit corn entirely, producing an intensely grain-forward character.

tableside: Heaven Hill's rye finished in toasted barrels — the same double-barrel technique as the Michter's bourbon, but applied to rye. The toasting adds sweetness and caramel depth that balances the spice nicely. A gentler entry point into the rye category.
story: Elijah Craig Rye takes Heaven Hill's approach to toasted barrel finishing and applies it to rye whiskey rather than bourbon. A second barrel that has been toasted rather than charred adds wood sugar sweetness through caramelization rather than the filtering and smokiness of a charred barrel. The result softens rye's naturally aggressive character while adding a dessert-like depth that makes it more approachable than most expressions in the category. Heaven Hill has been producing whiskey in Bardstown, Kentucky since 1935 and brings that institutional knowledge to even their less-publicized releases.
trivia: Heaven Hill is better known for bourbon than rye, but their rye program has grown significantly as the category has expanded — Elijah Craig Rye represents their most experimental approach to the grain.

Toasted barrel finishing adds different wood compounds than charred barrel aging — toast caramelizes wood sugars without creating the carbon filter layer that char produces, resulting in sweeter, less smoky wood influence.

The rye whiskey category has grown faster than any other American whiskey segment over the past decade, driven partly by the craft cocktail renaissance that put rye back in Manhattan and Old Fashioned recipes.
mash_bill: 51% rye, 35% corn, 14% malted barley
why: Heaven Hill's standard rye expression using a barely-legal 51% rye mash bill. The lower rye percentage produces a rounder, more bourbon-adjacent character that serves well as a Manhattan rye.

name: Midwinter's Night Dram
tableside: High West's winter seasonal — a blended rye finished in French oak port barrels, with additional finishing in sauternes, French oak, and other casks depending on the chapter. Rich, complex, and genuinely seasonal. One of the most anticipated annual rye releases.
story: High West Distillery in Park City, Utah was founded by David Perkins, a former biochemist, who studied the science of whiskey before building one of the first urban distilleries in the state since Prohibition. The Midwinter's Night Dram is an annual limited release designed to be the whiskey equivalent of a winter celebration, rich, warming, and complex enough to anchor a special occasion. The base is High West's Rendezvous Rye, finished in French oak port casks and then in French oak casks that previously held a rare Armagnac. Each annual chapter adds or modifies the finishing elements, making every vintage genuinely different from the last.
trivia: High West names each annual Midwinter's Night Dram release a 'chapter,' acknowledging the ongoing nature of the expression and the year-to-year variations in finishing.
The Rendezvous Rye base used for Midwinter's Night Dram blends ryes of different ages and mashbills — typically a 16-year Barton rye and a younger MGP rye — before the finishing program begins.
High West releases Midwinter's Night Dram in October, timed for the beginning of the holiday season, and it typically sells out within days of release.
mash_bill: Blend: 95% rye/5% barley (MGP) + 80% rye/20% malted rye (High West)
why: High West's annual limited release blends aged rye from multiple sources including their own distillery. The Midwinter's Night Dram is finished in a sequence of Port and French oak barrels.

name: Mardi Gras XO 11 Year
detail: Kentucky · 51.4% · Louisiana XO Rum Finish · 11 Year
tableside: The most expensive rye on the list — 11 years old, finished in Louisiana XO rum casks. Kentucky Owl is a prestige brand and this is their statement rye. The rum finish adds molasses and tropical character to a well-aged, serious grain whiskey.
story: Kentucky Owl is a luxury bourbon and rye brand revived by Dixon Dedman, whose family produced whiskey in Kentucky before Prohibition. The Mardi Gras XO expression celebrates Louisiana's French Caribbean heritage by finishing 11-year Kentucky rye in XO rum casks from Louisiana producers , a geographical and cultural bridge between Kentucky whiskey tradition and Gulf Coast spirits culture. XO rum casks carry particularly complex residue from extended rum aging, adding layers of tropical fruit and molasses that transform the already mature rye into something genuinely extraordinary.
trivia: XO rum must be aged a minimum of six years — the casks used for Kentucky Owl Mardi Gras carry residue from some of the most complex and aged rum available in Louisiana production.

Kentucky Owl was originally founded in 1935 by C.M. Dedman after Prohibition ended — the brand was revived by his great-grandson Dixon Dedman in 2015 after the family rediscovered the historical records.

Louisiana has a small but growing rum production industry tied to its sugarcane agriculture — the XO rum casks used for this expression represent genuine regional American craft rather than imported Caribbean barrels.
mash_bill: Undisclosed blend — sourced from multiple distilleries, 11 year
why: Kentucky Owl is a blending house that sources exceptional aged whiskey and assembles it without releasing the sources. The Mardi Gras XO 11 Year is a blend of aged ryes finished in XO Cognac barrels.

tableside: Knob Creek Rye at single barrel, full proof, Virginia select. At 57.5% the rye spice is genuinely intense — this is for guests who want to taste what rye is actually about. Great value for the proof and provenance.
story: Knob Creek Rye Single Barrel applies the same high-proof, full-flavored philosophy that defines the bourbon line to Kentucky rye whiskey. Jim Beam's distillery in Clermont has been producing Knob Creek since 1992, when master distiller Booker Noe created the brand as a reaction against the trend toward lighter, lower-proof American whiskey. The Virginia ABC select designation means state buyers hand-picked this specific barrel from the rye inventory. At barrel proof it is among the most intense expressions in the rye category, with the grain's natural spice amplified significantly by the proof level.
trivia: Knob Creek Rye uses a mashbill of 51% rye — the legal minimum to be called rye whiskey — combined with corn and malted barley, producing a less intensely spicy rye than MGP's 95% rye mashbill.
The lower rye percentage in Knob Creek's mashbill allows more corn sweetness to balance the rye spice, creating a more approachable profile than pure high-rye expressions despite the high proof.
Virginia ABC select barrel programs for rye are less common than for bourbon, making VA Select rye expressions like this one particularly noteworthy for Virginia whiskey enthusiasts.
mash_bill: 51% rye, 37% corn, 12% malted barley
why: Knob Creek Rye uses Jim Beam's 51% rye mash bill at 9 years old and 100 proof , the same standards Knob Creek applies to its bourbon. A full-flavored, approachable rye that works well in cocktails.

tableside: Michter's US*1 Rye is their entry single barrel rye — lower proof, approachable, with the house's characteristic smoothness. A good starting point for a guest curious about rye who doesn't want full intensity. Michter's climate-controlled aging produces unusually consistent results.
story: Michter's US*1 Rye is the entry expression in the distillery's rye lineup, a single barrel release at a deliberately approachable proof designed to showcase rye character without the intensity that can put off new drinkers. Michter's operates from a historic fort distillery site in Louisville and is known for low barrel entry proof and extended aging, practices that produce particularly even and consistent maturation. The US*1 Rye is released in small batches and reflects that philosophy at its most accessible. It is a reliable, well-made rye that makes a strong case for the category to guests who are not sure they like it.
trivia: Michter's US*1 designation refers to the distillery's commitment to producing single barrel expressions at every level of their lineup — 'US' for United States, '*1' for single barrel.
At 42.4% ABV, Michter's US*1 Rye is one of the lower-proof ryes on the list — a deliberate choice to emphasize grain character over alcohol intensity.
Michter's has been named 'American Whiskey Producer of the Year' multiple times by Whisky Advocate magazine, with the consistent quality of expressions like US*1 Rye cited as a primary factor.
mash_bill: Undisclosed — Kentucky rye, approximately 51% rye minimum
why: Michter's uses a Kentucky-style rye mash bill close to the legal minimum, but processes it with low barrel entry proof and extended aging. The result is a notably complex rye that defies its relatively low rye percentage.

name: Barrel Proof
tableside: The full proof version of Michter's US*1 Rye — same single barrel selection, same climate-controlled aging, no dilution. The difference between 42.4% and 55.25% is dramatic. The rye character is significantly more pronounced and the whole thing has a weight the standard expression doesn't.
story: Michter's Barrel Proof Rye offers a direct comparison to the standard US*1 Rye, same production philosophy and barrel selection standards, but bottled without the water addition that brings the regular expression to 42.4%. The additional proof reveals compounds that are present but muted in the diluted version, making it a genuinely instructive tasting experience for guests who want to understand what proof does to flavor. Michter's releases this in very limited quantities annually, making it one of the harder allocated ryes to encounter on a list. The rye spice in particular becomes significantly more assertive without dilution.
trivia: Michter's Barrel Proof Rye is released in small batches with no set schedule — it appears when barrels meeting the distillery's standards are ready, making each release genuinely limited.

The 12.85% ABV difference between Michter's US*1 Rye and the Barrel Proof version represents a significant concentration of flavor compounds — roughly speaking, there is proportionally more of everything in the higher-proof expression.
Michter's does not chill filter their barrel proof expressions, preserving oils and compounds that contribute to texture and mouthfeel at the cost of potential cloudiness at low temperatures.
mash_bill: Undisclosed — Kentucky rye, approximately 51% rye minimum
why: Michter's Barrel Proof Rye is released in very limited quantities at natural cask strength, uncut and unfiltered. The most intense expression in the Michter's rye lineup.

name: 10 Year
tableside: Ten years of aging softens the rye spice while adding depth that younger expressions can't approach. Michter's 10 Year Rye is one of the finest aged ryes in production — the grain character is still there but it's been refined by a decade of Kentucky oak.
story: Michter's 10 Year Single Barrel Rye represents the pinnacle of the distillery's rye program and is released in extremely limited quantities each year. A decade of aging in Michter's climate-controlled warehouses develops a complexity in rye whiskey that most producers never pursue. The grain's natural spice character evolves into something more refined, with dried fruit, leather, and deep oak joining the pepper and herb foundation. Master distiller Joe Magliocco has confirmed that no barrel in the Michter's 10 Year program, bourbon or rye, has ever been released under 11.5 years old, making the age statement conservative by design.
trivia: Aged rye whiskey is significantly rarer than aged bourbon because far fewer distilleries have maintained continuous rye production long enough to have 10-year inventory.
Michter's 10 Year Rye is released without a fixed schedule — the distillery waits until barrels have developed to their standard rather than pulling at a predetermined date.
The 10-year age statement on Michter's Rye represents a minimum — individual barrels within the release may have aged longer if they were deemed not yet ready at the 10-year mark.
mash_bill: Undisclosed — approximately 53% rye, 33% corn, 14% malted barley (rumored Brown-Forman recipe)
why: The rarest and oldest regular release in the Michter's rye lineup, aged at least 11.5 years by master distiller Joe Magliocco's confirmed standard. Released annually in very limited quantities.

tableside: Old Forester Rye at barrel proof — 64.15% is as intense as rye gets on this list. Dry, spicy, and powerful. For the guest who wants to experience rye at its most uncompromising. Water helps significantly.
story: Old Forester Rye Single Barrel Barrel Proof is Brown-Forman's most aggressive rye expression, the full character of their rye distillate at cask strength from a single barrel without blending or dilution. Brown-Forman has been making whiskey at their Shively, Kentucky distillery since the 1870s, and Old Forester remains America's longest continuously produced bourbon. Applying that institutional knowledge to rye produces something worth taking seriously. At over 64% ABV it is one of the highest-proof ryes commercially available, and at its price point it represents one of the better barrel proof values on the list.
trivia: Old Forester's rye whiskey program is a relatively recent addition compared to the brand's 150+ year bourbon history, reflecting the broader industry expansion into rye as consumer demand has grown.
At 64.15% ABV, Old Forester Rye Barrel Proof is among the highest-proof commercial rye expressions available in the US market — only a handful of limited releases exceed this level.
Brown-Forman's decision to release a barrel proof rye reflects the company's recognition that rye drinkers skew toward high-proof, uncompromising expressions more than the average bourbon consumer.
mash_bill: 72% corn, 18% rye, 10% malted barley (Old Forester rye uses same distillery recipe)
why: Old Forester's rye uses the same Brown-Forman distillery as the bourbon lineup. A 100-proof, bottled-in-bond rye that is one of the best values in the category.

name: 17th Edition
tableside: Parker's Heritage is Heaven Hill's annual prestige release, named for legendary master distiller Parker Beam. The 17th Edition is a 10-year rye at 64.4% — among the most serious aged ryes anywhere. Parker's Heritage raises money for ALS research in his memory.
story: Parker's Heritage Collection is Heaven Hill's annual limited release series, named for Parker Beam who served as master distiller for over 30 years before being diagnosed with ALS in 2012. Each release donates a portion of proceeds to ALS research, making every bottle both a prestige whiskey purchase and a charitable act. The 17th Edition is a 10-year rye at full barrel proof , Parker Beam's own preference for high-proof, uncompromising whiskey expressed through the grain he worked with throughout his career. It is among the finest and most emotionally significant releases in American whiskey.
trivia: Parker Beam was diagnosed with ALS (Lou Gehrig's Disease) in 2012 and passed away in 2017 — the Parker's Heritage Collection raises funds for ALS research in his memory with each annual release.
Parker Beam represented the seventh generation of the Beam family in whiskey, a lineage that has shaped American bourbon more profoundly than any other family in the industry's history.
Heaven Hill's ability to release a 10-year barrel proof rye reflects their extraordinary aging inventory — one of the deepest and most varied in American whiskey production.
mash_bill: Undisclosed — Heaven Hill wheated recipe variant
why: Parker's Heritage is Heaven Hill's annual limited collector's series. The 17th edition is a rare wheated rye expression , using wheat as the flavoring grain rather than the standard corn.

detail: Pennsylvania · 50% · Bottled in Bond · VA Select
tableside: Rittenhouse is the bartender's rye — 100 proof, Bottled in Bond, and the foundation of countless great Manhattans and Old Fashioneds. At $11 it's the best value pour on the rye list and a benchmark for the Pennsylvania rye style.
story: Rittenhouse Rye is named after Rittenhouse Square in Philadelphia, a nod to the Pennsylvania rye tradition that dominated American whiskey before Prohibition. Now produced by Heaven Hill in Kentucky using a traditional rye mashbill, it has become the benchmark rye for the cocktail industry , bartenders from New York to Los Angeles reach for Rittenhouse when building a Manhattan or a Vieux Carré. At 100 proof and $11 a glass, it's the rare whiskey that is simultaneously a critical darling and a genuine value pour. The VA Select designation means Virginia buyers curated this specific barrel.
trivia: Rittenhouse Rye won 'Best American Whiskey' at the Tales of the Cocktail Spirited Awards multiple times, cementing its status as the bartender's benchmark rye.
Pennsylvania rye whiskey was America's most prestigious whiskey style before Prohibition — the Monongahela rye tradition produced dry, spicy expressions that defined the category for generations.
Rittenhouse Square in Philadelphia, for which the whiskey is named, was laid out by William Penn in 1682 as one of five original squares in the city's founding plan.
mash_bill: 51% rye, 37% corn, 12% malted barley
why: The benchmark American rye whiskey. Rittenhouse Bottled in Bond is 100 proof, at least 4 years old, and uses Heaven Hill's 51% rye mash bill. The standard against which cocktail ryes are measured.

tableside: Thomas Handy is part of the Buffalo Trace Antique Collection — released once a year, among the most allocated spirits in American whiskey. Named for the man who popularized the Sazerac cocktail, it's the definitive expression of high-proof Kentucky rye. A serious pour.
story: Thomas Handy Sazerac Rye is part of the Buffalo Trace Antique Collection , an annual fall release alongside George T. Stagg, Eagle Rare 17, Sazerac 18, and William Larue Weller. The collection represents the most anticipated release in American whiskey each year, with individual bottles often selling for multiples of retail on the secondary market. Named for Thomas H. Handy, a New Orleans bartender who popularized the Sazerac cocktail in the late 1800s, it's released at full barrel proof without dilution , the most intense expression of the Buffalo Trace rye distillate available to the public.
trivia: The Sazerac cocktail — rye whiskey, Peychaud's bitters, absinthe rinse, sugar — is considered by many to be America's oldest cocktail, originating in New Orleans in the mid-1800s.

Thomas H. Handy acquired the Sazerac Coffee House in New Orleans in 1869, where he served and popularized the cocktail that now bears his name — Buffalo Trace honors this legacy with their most prized rye.

The Buffalo Trace Antique Collection is released in the fall of each year and allocated through a lottery system in most states — finding Thomas Handy at retail requires significant luck or relationships.
mash_bill: ~55% rye, ~41% corn, ~4% malted barley (BT rye mash bill, undisclosed)
why: Part of Buffalo Trace's annual Antique Collection , one of the most sought-after rye releases in the world. Uncut, unfiltered, and released once a year in very limited quantities.

name: Rare Breed
tableside: Wild Turkey Rare Breed Rye at barrel proof — $20 for a full proof Kentucky rye from one of the great houses. Wild Turkey's characteristic boldness applied to rye produces something assertive and genuinely impressive for the price.
story: Wild Turkey Rare Breed Rye extends the barrel proof philosophy of the bourbon Rare Breed into the rye category , a blended barrel proof expression bottled without dilution to show Wild Turkey's rye distillate at full strength. The distillery's characteristic approach , bold fermentation, high char barrels, Kentucky climate exposure , produces rye with more backbone and tannic structure than most producers achieve. At $20 for barrel proof rye from a distillery with this heritage, it represents exceptional value in a category where serious proof typically commands serious prices.
trivia: Wild Turkey Rare Breed Rye was introduced relatively recently compared to the bourbon version, reflecting the brand's recognition that their bold house style translates powerfully to the rye category.

Wild Turkey's rye mashbill uses a higher percentage of rye than their bourbon — the exact recipe is proprietary, but the resulting spice character is noticeably more intense than the bourbon's rye contribution.
Eddie Russell, who oversees Wild Turkey's production alongside his father Jimmy, has been particularly involved in developing the distillery's rye program as the category has grown.
mash_bill: 51% rye, 37% corn, 12% malted barley
why: Wild Turkey's cask strength rye, released in limited batches throughout the year. Uses the same 51% rye mash bill as standard Wild Turkey Rye but at natural barrel proof.

name: 15 Year
tableside: Whistlepig 15 Year is the benchmark aged rye in American whiskey — fifteen years old, Vermont aging, serious complexity. The spice of a great rye with the depth and integration that only significant age can provide. One of the finest pours on the list.
story: Whistlepig Farm in Shoreham, Vermont sits on 500 acres and has become one of the most celebrated addresses in American rye whiskey. The 15 Year is sourced from aged Canadian rye stocks that the distillery has carefully selected, barreled, and aged further on the Vermont farm. Fifteen years of aging produces rye with a complexity rarely seen in the category , the grain's natural spice character has been refined by time into something more nuanced, with fruit, chocolate, and leather notes joining the pepper foundation. It is frequently cited as the finest commercially available aged rye in America.
trivia: Whistlepig Farm was established by Raj Bhakta, who purchased the Vermont property in 2007 specifically to grow rye grain and build a farm-to-bottle rye whiskey operation.
The rye whiskey category's resurgence since 2010 has been partly driven by Whistlepig's success in positioning aged rye as a premium, terroir-driven spirit comparable to fine Scotch.
Vermont's cooler climate means aging proceeds more slowly than in Kentucky — 15 years in Vermont may produce similar wood extraction to 10–12 years in a Kentucky rick house.
mash_bill: 100% rye (sourced from Alberta Distillers, Canada)
why: WhistlePig 15 Year is sourced from Alberta Distillers in Canada and uses 100% rye , no corn, no barley. One of the oldest and rarest standard releases in the WhistlePig lineup.

name: 18 Year
tableside: Eighteen years is extraordinary for rye whiskey — the 15 Year is already exceptional, but the 18 Year adds three more years of Vermont aging that push the complexity into territory most ryes never reach. The most serious rye pour on the list at $100.
story: Whistlepig 18 Year pushes aged rye whiskey to the outer limit of what extended oak contact can achieve. Three additional years beyond the already impressive 15 Year add layers of dried fruit, leather, and deep wood character that only come with serious time. At $100 a glass it is the most expensive rye on the list, but it represents a genuinely rare category of whiskey , rye with the kind of age typically associated with prestige Scotch expressions. For guests who appreciate aged spirits at a high level, the Whistlepig 18 Year is a significant and memorable pour.
trivia: 18-year-old rye whiskey is extraordinarily rare in commercial production — most rye is released between 4 and 12 years, making the Whistlepig 18 Year an outlier in terms of maturation.
Whistlepig sources its aged rye from Alberta, Canada, where the Alberta Distillers Ltd. has maintained stocks of very old rye that are among the rarest aged grain spirits in North America.

The price premium between Whistlepig 15 Year and 18 Year reflects both the rarity of 18-year rye inventory and the additional three years of warehouse carrying costs, evaporation losses, and capital tied up in aging barrels.
mash_bill: 79% rye, 15% malted rye, 6% malted barley (Alberta Distillers, Canada)
why: The WhistlePig 18 Year uses a unique mash bill from Alberta Distillers: 79% rye, 15% malted rye, and 6% malted barley. The double-malt rye produces a distinctly different character from standard rye whiskey.

name: Family Estate Rye 4 Year
tableside: Willet Family Estate is a small Kentucky distillery producing rye from their own grain at their own facility — genuinely farm-to-bottle. At four years and full barrel proof, it's young and assertive, but the craftsmanship is evident. A different kind of rye from the big-distillery expressions.
story: Willet Distillery in Bardstown, Kentucky is a family operation that spent years as a bottler and blender before investing in their own distillation program. The Family Estate Rye is made from grain grown on or near the distillery property, distilled at Willet, and aged in their own warehouses , a level of vertical integration that is genuinely unusual in bourbon country. At four years old it's a young whiskey, but the quality of the distillate and the care of the aging program produce something that rewards attention despite its youth. Each barrel is bottled individually at full proof.
trivia: Willet Distillery was founded in 1935 by Lambert Willet and has remained in the Kulsveen family — who married into the Willet family — through multiple generations of Kentucky whiskey history.

For decades, Willet operated primarily as a non-distillery producer, sourcing and bottling other distilleries' whiskey under the Willet name — a practice that built their reputation before their own distillate came of age.

The Family Estate designation on Willet bottles guarantees that the whiskey was produced entirely at the Bardstown distillery from grain through bottling — a significant commitment in a state where sourcing is common.
mash_bill: Undisclosed — distilled at Willet, pot still single barrel rye
why: Distilled at the Willet Distillery using a pot still , rare for American rye whiskey. Single barrel, bottled at natural cask strength, with each barrel producing a genuinely different whiskey.

tableside: Woodford Reserve Rye VA Select — Virginia buyers picked this single barrel from Brown-Forman's rye program. At 54.2% it shows the full character of Woodford's triple-distilled rye distillate. Great value for a named distillery single barrel at $15.
story: Woodford Reserve Rye applies the distillery's triple distillation process, genuinely unusual in American whiskey, to rye grain, producing a cleaner and more refined rye distillate than column still alternatives. The historic Labrot and Graham Distillery in Versailles, Kentucky uses copper pot stills alongside a column still, a combination that produces the smooth, rounded character Woodford is known for across all its expressions. The Virginia ABC single barrel select program adds a layer of curation, with state buyers tasting through available barrels and choosing this specific one for Virginia distribution.
trivia: Woodford Reserve uses copper pot stills for triple distillation — a method borrowed from Scottish single malt production that removes more congeners and produces a lighter, more refined distillate.
Woodford's triple distillation approach applied to rye grain creates a noticeably different flavor profile than column-still ryes, with more delicate grain character and less aggressive spice.

Virginia ABC select barrel programs have expanded significantly in recent years, with state buyers now visiting dozens of distilleries annually to curate exclusive single barrels across bourbon, rye, and other categories.
mash_bill: 53% rye, 33% corn, 14% malted barley
why: Woodford Reserve's rye is distilled at the historic Woodford Reserve Distillery using pot stills alongside column stills. One of the only ryes made with this combination of equipment.

name: 10 Year
tableside: Bushmills 10 Year is the entry point into Irish single malt — triple distilled, ten years old, approachable and gentle. For a guest who finds Scotch too smoky or bourbon too sweet, Irish whiskey is often the answer and this is the right place to start.
story: Old Bushmills Distillery in County Antrim, Northern Ireland holds a claim to being the world's oldest licensed distillery, with a license granted in 1608, though distilling on the site likely predates that by centuries. The distillery uses 100% malted barley and triple distillation in copper pot stills, a combination that produces a lighter, more approachable spirit than the double distillation used in Scotch production. The water comes from Saint Columb's Rill, a tributary of the River Bush, and has been the source for every bottle since the beginning. The 10 Year represents the entry point to the Bushmills aged single malt range.
trivia: Bushmills holds a Royal Warrant dating to 1608 when King James I granted a license to distill in the area — making it one of the oldest documented whiskey producers in the world.
Irish whiskey law requires triple distillation in copper pot stills for single malt expressions, producing a lighter, more delicate spirit than the typically double-distilled Scotch single malt.
The Old Bushmills Distillery sits on the banks of the Saint Columb's Rill, a tributary of the River Bush — the local water source that has defined the whiskey's character for centuries.
mash_bill: 100% malted barley (single malt, triple distilled at Bushmills)
why: Bushmills 10 Year is a single malt , 100% malted barley, triple distilled, matured in a combination of bourbon and sherry casks. One of the most accessible aged Irish single malts available.

name: 12 Year
tableside: Two more years and a sherry cask influence lifts the 10 Year into more complex territory — the fruit and malt are still there but with more depth and a light dried fruit character from the sherry wood. A meaningful step up for $3 more.
story: Bushmills 12 Year is matured in a combination of bourbon and sherry casks before a final period in Oloroso sherry barrels, adding layers of dried fruit and subtle sweetness to the malt foundation that defines the 10 Year. The Old Bushmills Distillery in County Antrim is the only distillery in Northern Ireland producing single malt Irish whiskey, giving it a geographic uniqueness within the category. The additional aging and sherry influence represent a meaningful evolution from the entry expression, same triple-distilled lightness but with more complexity and a longer finish.
trivia: Bushmills 12 Year uses Oloroso sherry casks for finishing — Oloroso is a dry, oxidatively aged sherry that contributes dried fruit, nut, and dark spice notes to the whiskey.
The combination of ex-bourbon and ex-sherry maturation is common in Irish whiskey production, reflecting both the availability of these cask types and their complementary flavor contributions.
Bushmills 12 Year is produced in relatively larger quantities than the older expressions in the lineup, making it the most accessible age-stated Bushmills expression for most markets.
mash_bill: 100% malted barley (single malt, triple distilled at Bushmills)
why: Twelve years in bourbon and sherry casks, triple distilled. The Bushmills 12 Year adds the influence of oloroso sherry to the standard 10 Year profile , more dried fruit and spice.

name: 16 Year
detail: Northern Ireland · 40% · Irish Single Malt · Port Cask Finish
tableside: Sixteen years with a port cask finish — the oldest widely available Bushmills expression and the one that shows what Irish single malt looks like with serious age and European wood influence. Rich, fruity, and substantially more complex than the 12 Year.
story: Bushmills 16 Year is the most complex expression in the standard lineup, matured in a combination of bourbon barrels and Oloroso sherry casks before a final period in port barrels. Sixteen years of triple-distilled development in Northern Ireland's cool, damp climate produces something genuinely impressive. The lightness that characterizes younger Bushmills expressions has been transformed by time into depth without weight, with fruit and spice complexity that rivals far more expensive European whisky expressions. The port finishing adds a dark cherry richness that makes it the most distinctive pour in the Bushmills range.
trivia: Port cask finishing adds anthocyanins from the fortified wine residue, contributing deep red fruit notes and a slight pink tinge that distinguishes port-finished whiskey from other cask finishes.
At 16 years, Bushmills has aged longer than most Irish whiskeys on the market — the category has historically favored younger expressions compared to Scotch, making this an outlier in age terms.
The three-cask maturation process — bourbon, sherry, port — used for Bushmills 16 Year is one of the more complex aging programs in Irish whiskey production.
mash_bill: 100% malted barley (single malt, triple distilled at Bushmills)
why: The Bushmills 16 Year is matured in three cask types: bourbon, oloroso sherry, and port. The port finish in the final years adds red fruit and a subtle sweetness that makes this one of the most complex Irish single malts.

name: 21 Year
detail: Northern Ireland · 46% · Irish Single Malt · Madeira Finish
tableside: Twenty-one years finished in Madeira casks — this is Bushmills at its most serious. Madeira's oxidative complexity adds dried fruit and nutty depth to two decades of Irish single malt development. One of the finest Irish whiskeys available anywhere.
story: Bushmills 21 Year is the pinnacle of the distillery's standard lineup , two decades of triple-distilled Irish single malt development, finished in Madeira casks that have held one of the world's most complex fortified wines. Madeira's extraordinary aging capacity and oxidative character , dried fruit, nut, caramel complexity that develops over decades , transfers into the whiskey during finishing, adding a European sophistication that makes Bushmills 21 Year genuinely comparable to the finest aged Scotch expressions. At $90, it is exceptional value for what it delivers.
trivia: Madeira wine is produced on the Portuguese island of the same name and is famous for surviving centuries — bottles from the 1700s and 1800s are still drinkable, making the casks uniquely complex finishing vessels.
Bushmills 21 Year is bottled at 46% rather than the 40% of younger expressions — a proof point chosen to preserve the full complexity developed over two decades of aging.

The combination of triple distillation and Madeira finishing creates a whiskey that occupies a unique stylistic space — lighter than Scotch but more complex than younger Irish expressions, with European wine characteristics that make it accessible to non-whiskey drinkers.
mash_bill: 100% malted barley (single malt, triple distilled at Bushmills)
why: Bushmills 21 Year is triple distilled and finished in Madeira wine casks , an unusual finishing choice that adds nutty, dried fruit, and slightly oxidative notes. One of the oldest regularly available Irish single malts.

tableside: Green Spot is one of Ireland's most beloved single pot still whiskeys — a style unique to Ireland that uses a mix of malted and unmalted barley. Fruity, fresh, and immediately approachable. A great introduction to Irish whiskey's most distinctive native style.
story: Green Spot is produced by Irish Distillers at Midleton Distillery in County Cork and is one of the most important expressions of Irish single pot still whiskey , a category unique to Ireland where both malted and unmalted barley are used in the still. The unmalted barley contributes a creamier texture and a distinctive spicy, oily character that sets pot still Irish whiskey apart from both single malt and grain whiskey styles. Green Spot was historically sold exclusively through Mitchell & Son, a Dublin wine merchant, and carries a sense of place and tradition that makes it a genuine conversation piece.
trivia: Single pot still Irish whiskey — using a mix of malted and unmalted barley in a copper pot still — is a style unique to Ireland and is legally protected as a distinctly Irish production method.
Green Spot was historically matured exclusively in sherry and bourbon barrels supplied by Mitchell & Son, a Dublin wine and spirits merchant who has been selling the whiskey since the 1800s.
The 'Spot' whiskies — Green, Yellow, Red, and Blue — are named after the spots of colored paint that Mitchell & Son used to mark barrels of different ages in their bonded warehouse.
mash_bill: Single pot still: minimum 30% malted barley, 30% unmalted barley, remainder mixed grain (Midleton)
why: Green Spot is produced at Midleton Distillery in Cork as a single pot still Irish whiskey , a style native to Ireland made from a blend of malted and unmalted barley. Matured exclusively in bourbon casks.

tableside: Gold Spot is the premium sibling of Green Spot — nine years old and bottled at full strength. The extra age and higher proof reveal the full depth of Irish single pot still whiskey. One of the most interesting and underappreciated pours on the entire list.
story: Gold Spot is the premium expression in the Spot Whiskey family, aged nine years and bottled at full strength without dilution , a significant departure from the standard Green Spot in terms of both age and intensity. The pot still whiskey style, with its characteristic mix of malted and unmalted barley, develops beautifully with extended aging , the creamy, oily texture of younger pot still whiskey transforms into something richer and more complex while maintaining the apple and honey character that defines the family. At $75 for a full-strength, nine-year Irish pot still whiskey, it is genuinely exceptional value.
