trivia: Gold Spot was historically a 12-year whiskey in the mid-twentieth century before production was discontinued — the modern revival uses a 9-year age statement reflecting current stock availability.
The 51.4% ABV of Gold Spot is a natural cask strength — the whiskey is bottled without dilution at the proof the barrels produced after nine years of aging.
Mitchell & Son, the Dublin merchant behind the Spot Whiskey range, has been in business since 1805 — making it one of the oldest wine and spirits merchants in Ireland.
mash_bill: Single pot still: minimum 30% malted barley, 30% unmalted barley, remainder mixed grain (Midleton)
why: Gold Spot is Green Spot's premium expression, aged 9 years in a combination of bourbon, sherry, and Marsala wine casks. Named after the original Mitchell & Son wine merchant's labeling system from the 19th century.

tableside: Jameson is the world's best-selling Irish whiskey and the entry point for most guests into the category. Smooth, approachable, and never wrong. At $10 it's the most accessible spirit on the list and a reliable answer to any guest who wants something easy.
story: Jameson Irish Whiskey is produced at the Midleton Distillery in County Cork , the largest and most important whiskey facility in Ireland. It is a blended Irish whiskey combining single pot still and grain whiskeys, triple distilled for smoothness, and aged in a combination of ex-bourbon and ex-sherry casks. The result is a whiskey specifically engineered to be universally approachable , light, slightly sweet, and devoid of the challenging characteristics that make other whiskey styles polarizing. It is the world's fastest-growing major spirits brand and the gateway Irish whiskey for millions of drinkers annually.
trivia: Jameson is the third best-selling whiskey in the world by volume, behind only Johnnie Walker and Jack Daniel's — it sells over 10 million cases per year globally.

John Jameson, a Scottish lawyer, founded his Dublin distillery in 1780 — his family's whiskey became so successful that the distillery eventually merged with others to form Irish Distillers, which Pernod Ricard acquired in 1988.

Jameson's triple distillation process removes congeners that contribute to harsh flavors in other whiskey styles — the result is a spirit smooth enough to drink neat, on the rocks, or in cocktails without the grain character dominating.
mash_bill: Blended: single pot still and grain whiskey (Midleton Distillery, Cork)
why: The world's best-selling Irish whiskey. A blend of pot still and grain whiskey from Midleton Distillery, triple distilled for smoothness. The reference point for Irish whiskey globally.

name: 10 Year
tableside: Ardbeg 10 Year is the benchmark heavily peated Islay Scotch — intense smoke, sea salt, and underlying sweetness that makes it more than just a one-note experience. For a guest who has never had a seriously smoky whisky, this is the definitive introduction.
story: Ardbeg Distillery sits on the southern shore of Islay, a small island off Scotland's west coast that produces some of the world's most intensely peated whisky. The distillery was founded in 1815 and has changed hands multiple times, nearly closing permanently in 1996 before being acquired by Glenmorangie , now owned by LVMH , which restored it to full production. Ardbeg 10 Year is bottled at 46% without chill filtration, preserving the full intensity of the distillate's peat smoke, maritime brine, and underlying vanilla sweetness. It is the reference point for heavily peated Scotch.
trivia: Ardbeg uses barley peated to approximately 55 parts per million of phenols — one of the highest peat levels of any commercial Scotch distillery, producing the intensely smoky character the brand is known for.

Islay's peat bogs contain decomposed plant matter including seaweed and moss, which contributes the distinctive maritime, medicinal quality to Islay peated whisky that is absent from mainland peated expressions.
Ardbeg 10 Year is bottled at 46% and without chill filtration — preserving the oily mouthfeel and full aromatic complexity that chill filtration would partially remove.
mash_bill: 100% malted barley (heavily peated, ~55 PPM, distilled at Ardbeg, Islay)
why: Ardbeg 10 Year is the entry point into one of Islay's most iconic distilleries. Peated at approximately 55 PPM , among the highest levels of any standard expression , it is the benchmark heavily peated Scotch whisky.

name: Uigeadail
detail: Islay · 54.2% · Single Malt Scotch · Oloroso Sherry Finish
tableside: Uigeadail — pronounced 'oog-a-dal' — adds Oloroso sherry cask maturation to Ardbeg's peat smoke. The result is the most complex Ardbeg expression: smoke and chocolate and dried fruit simultaneously. Named after the loch that supplies Ardbeg's water.
story: Ardbeg Uigeadail is named after Loch Uigeadail, the remote mountain loch that supplies the distillery's water , a fitting name for an expression that feels like it comes from a particularly dramatic and uncompromising place. The combination of Ardbeg's heavily peated distillate with Oloroso sherry cask maturation is one of the great pairings in Scotch whisky: the smoke and the dark fruit amplify each other rather than competing, producing a complexity that makes it one of the most awarded whiskies in the world. At cask strength and $27, it is among the best values on the list.
trivia: Ardbeg Uigeadail has won 'World Whisky of the Year' from Jim Murray's Whisky Bible — the industry's most widely read annual guide — earning it global recognition that drove significant demand.

Oloroso sherry is a dry, oxidatively aged sherry that has been exposed to air throughout maturation — the resulting complex dried fruit, nut, and dark spice character makes it the most commonly used sherry cask type in Scotch whisky production.

Loch Uigeadail sits in remote moorland above the distillery — the water that flows from it through peat bogs picks up the same compounds that define Islay's whisky character before it reaches the distillery.
mash_bill: 100% malted barley (heavily peated, ~55 PPM, distilled at Ardbeg, Islay)
why: Named after the loch that supplies Ardbeg's water. Uigeadail blends heavily peated spirit from different ages with whisky matured in oloroso sherry casks, producing a rare combination of smoke and dried fruit. Consistently rated among the world's best whiskies.

name: Spectacular
detail: Islay · 46% · Single Malt Scotch · Port Cask Finish
tableside: Ardbeg Spectacular finishes in port casks — dark fruit and red berry character laid over the classic Ardbeg smoke and brine. The contrast between Islay peat and port wine sweetness is dramatic and genuinely surprising. The most approachable Ardbeg on the list for guests new to peated Scotch.
story: Ardbeg Spectacular is a port cask finished expression that takes the distillery's signature heavily peated character and adds the sweetness and dark fruit of port wine maturation. The result softens Ardbeg's most challenging edges while preserving the smoke and maritime character that defines the distillery , making it the most accessible entry point into heavily peated Islay Scotch for guests who might find the 10 Year overwhelming. The port cask finish creates a dramatic contrast that works precisely because both elements are strong enough to coexist without either overwhelming the other.
trivia: Port cask finishing is relatively uncommon for heavily peated Islay whiskies — the conventional wisdom is that delicate cask finishes are overwhelmed by intense peat, but Ardbeg's high phenol levels are robust enough to hold their own against port's sweetness.

The Ardbeg Spectacular name is a departure from the distillery's usual naming conventions — most Ardbeg expressions are named after geographical features of Islay or Gaelic terms related to the distillery.
Ardbeg releases several annual limited editions alongside the core range, and Spectacular represents a newer addition to the permanent lineup after beginning as a limited release.
mash_bill: 100% malted barley (heavily peated, ~55 PPM, distilled at Ardbeg, Islay)
why: Ardbeg Spectacular is a limited release blending new make from Ardbeg's recently commissioned second still with mature whisky from the original still. A rare opportunity to taste two generations of Ardbeg production side by side.

name: 12 Year
tableside: Glenfiddich 12 Year is the world's best-selling single malt Scotch — not because it's the most complex, but because it's the most consistently approachable. Fresh fruit, light honey, no rough edges. The right answer for a guest who wants Scotch without the challenge.
story: Glenfiddich Distillery was founded on Christmas Day 1887 by William Grant in Dufftown, Speyside, with equipment purchased second-hand from the Cardhu Distillery. It remains family-owned across five generations, a genuine rarity among major Scotch producers in an era of consolidation. The 12 Year is matured in a combination of European oak sherry casks and American oak bourbon barrels, producing the clean fruit and cream character that has made it the world's best-selling single malt Scotch whisky for decades. Speyside is Scotland's most prolific whisky-producing region, defined by gentle, fruity, non-peated expressions, and Glenfiddich 12 Year is the archetype of that style.
trivia: Glenfiddich is the world's best-selling single malt Scotch whisky, selling over 14 million bottles annually — roughly one in every five bottles of single malt consumed globally.
William Grant built Glenfiddich Distillery largely by hand with his sons, reportedly hauling stones from a nearby field — the family has maintained ownership for over 135 years.
Glenfiddich means 'Valley of the Deer' in Scottish Gaelic — the stag that appears on the label is a direct reference to the distillery's name and the wildlife of Speyside.
mash_bill: 100% malted barley (unpeated, distilled at Glenfiddich, Speyside)
why: Glenfiddich 12 Year is the world's best-selling single malt Scotch whisky. Matured in American oak and European oak casks, it defines the approachable, fruit-forward Speyside style.

name: 10 Year
tableside: The Glenlivet 10 Year is the most approachable and affordable Scotch on the list — floral, light, and easy to recommend to anyone new to the category. Glenlivet essentially invented the legal Scotch industry and this is the entry point to that legacy.
story: The Glenlivet has a unique place in Scotch whisky history: in 1824, George Smith became the first distiller in the Scottish Highlands to obtain a legal license under the Excise Act of 1823, which regularized the previously illicit trade. His whiskey became so popular and widely imitated that competitors began calling their products 'Glenlivet' , leading to a legal battle that eventually gave George Smith's distillery the exclusive right to call itself 'The Glenlivet.' The 10 Year expression is the most accessible entry point to this historic name, with the light floral character typical of upper Speyside.
trivia: The Glenlivet was the first licensed distillery in the Scottish Highlands following the Excise Act of 1823 — before this, Highland distilling was largely illegal and the industry operated in hiding.

The legal battle over the name 'Glenlivet' resulted in a court ruling that only George Smith's distillery could use 'The Glenlivet' — other Speyside producers who had been using the name had to add a hyphenated prefix.
King George IV visited Scotland in 1822 and specifically requested to drink Highland whisky — reportedly Glenlivet — helping legitimize the category after decades of government suppression.
mash_bill: 100% malted barley (unpeated, distilled at Glenlivet, Speyside)
why: Glenlivet 10 Year is one of the youngest expressions from one of Scotland's most historic distilleries , the first legally licensed distillery in the Scottish Highlands, established in 1824.

name: 10 Year
tableside: Laphroaig is the most divisive whisky on the list — guests either love it immediately or find it completely overwhelming. The iodine and medicinal quality is unlike anything else. For a guest who wants to understand why people are passionate about peated Scotch, this is the pour.
story: Laphroaig Distillery has been producing whisky on the southern shore of Islay since 1815, and its expression is one of the most recognizable and polarizing in all of Scotch whisky. The distinctive medicinal, iodine, and seaweed character comes from Islay's peat, which contains a high proportion of decomposed sea vegetation unique to the island. Laphroaig still malts a portion of its own barley on traditional floor maltings, one of the few distilleries to maintain this labor-intensive practice. Prince Charles awarded the distillery a Royal Warrant in 1994, making Laphroaig the only spirit to hold that honor, and he reportedly drinks it regularly.
trivia: Laphroaig is the only Scotch whisky to hold a Royal Warrant from Prince Charles — now King Charles III — who has been a publicly declared fan for decades.
The distillery maintains traditional floor maltings where barley is spread and turned by hand — a labor-intensive process largely abandoned by most distilleries in favor of industrial maltsters.

Laphroaig's 'Friends of Laphroaig' program allows whisky enthusiasts to 'own' a square foot of Islay land near the distillery — over 700,000 people have joined, receiving a rent of one dram per year on visits.
mash_bill: 100% malted barley (heavily peated, ~40-45 PPM, distilled at Laphroaig, Islay)
why: Laphroaig 10 Year is among the most intensely peated single malts available, with a distinctive medicinal, iodine, and seaweed character that comes from malting barley over peat from the Islay moss.

name: 12 Year
detail: Speyside · 40% · Single Malt Scotch · Sherry Cask
tableside: Macallan 12 Year Sherry Cask is the definitive sherry-matured Scotch — rich, fruity, and immediately impressive to guests who think they don't like whisky. The sherry influence dominates and transforms the spirit into something closer to dried fruit and chocolate than grain. One of the most popular pours on any premium list.
story: The Macallan is produced in Speyside on an estate that has grown barley for distilling since 1700. The modern distillery was founded in 1824 and is now one of the most valuable Scotch whisky brands in the world. Individual bottles of old Macallan have sold for hundreds of thousands of dollars at auction. The 12 Year Sherry Cask is matured exclusively in hand-selected sherry-seasoned oak casks sourced from Jerez, Spain, giving it the intense dried fruit, chocolate, and spice character that defines the Macallan house style. For guests who are new to Scotch or think they don't like whisky, this sherry-forward expression is consistently the most successful introduction.
trivia: The Macallan sources its sherry casks directly from cooperages in Jerez, Spain, where they are first used to season Oloroso sherry before being sent to Scotland — the distillery controls this process more rigorously than any other Scotch producer.
A bottle of The Macallan 60 Year sold at auction in 2019 for £1.5 million — the highest price ever paid for a single bottle of whisky at the time.

The Easter Elchies estate where Macallan is produced has been associated with distilling since 1824, when the first legal license was granted — the manor house that appears on the label dates to 1700.
mash_bill: 100% malted barley (unpeated, distilled at Macallan, Speyside)
why: Macallan 12 Year is matured exclusively in sherry-seasoned oak casks , the defining characteristic of Macallan and the reason for its distinctive dried fruit, spice, and chocolate notes.

name: Muckety Muck 25 Year
tableside: Muckety Muck is a 25-year single grain Scotch — not single malt, single grain, which means it's made from wheat or corn rather than malted barley. Lighter and sweeter than malt whisky, but with 25 years of oak depth. A genuinely unusual and underappreciated pour.
story: Orphan Barrel is a project by Diageo that rescues forgotten or orphaned casks of aged whisky from warehouses around the world and bottles them under creative names. Muckety Muck 25 Year is a single grain Scotch , produced from wheat or corn in a continuous column still rather than the pot still malted barley production of single malt. Single grain Scotch is a significantly underappreciated category: aged for a quarter century, it develops oak complexity and richness while maintaining a lighter, sweeter character than malt whisky. The playful name belies the seriousness of what's in the bottle.
trivia: Single grain Scotch whisky is produced in a continuous column still using grains other than or in addition to malted barley — typically wheat or corn — producing a lighter, more neutral spirit than pot still malt whisky.

The Orphan Barrel project has rescued aged whiskey and whisky from multiple countries, including the US, Canada, and Scotland — aging inventory that was either forgotten or produced by distilleries that have since closed.
At 25 years old, Muckety Muck is the oldest expression on the Scotch list — and one of the oldest spirits poured by the glass anywhere in the region at its price point.
mash_bill: 100% malted barley (distilled at Strathmill, Speyside — 25 year orphan barrel release)
why: Orphan Barrel Muckety Muck is whisky distilled at the now-demolished Strathmill Distillery in Speyside and aged for 25 years. It is one of the oldest single malts on the list and cannot be reproduced.

tableside: Bo and Ivy is an unaged corn whiskey — essentially legal moonshine from Virginia. No barrel time, just pure distilled corn character. Fresh, clean, and surprisingly interesting. The most unusual pour on the list and a great conversation starter.
story: Bo and Ivy is produced in Virginia and represents the most elemental form of American grain spirit , corn whiskey that goes directly from the still to the bottle without any barrel aging. Without oak to add color, vanilla, and caramel, what remains is the pure character of the grain: fresh corn sweetness, floral notes, and a clean distillate quality that reveals the craft of the still. It occupies the same legal and historical space as moonshine , the unaged whiskey that American distillers have produced since the colonial era , but made with modern precision.
trivia: Unaged corn whiskey is legally distinct from bourbon, which must be aged in new charred oak — corn whiskey can be sold immediately after distillation or aged in used or uncharred barrels.
Virginia has a long history of corn whiskey production dating to the colonial era, when corn was the most abundant grain available to distillers in the mid-Atlantic region.

The absence of barrel aging in Bo and Ivy means the color, aroma, and flavor come entirely from the grain and the distillation process — a transparency that reveals the distiller's craft without wood to mask imperfections.
mash_bill: Undisclosed American whiskey blend
why: Bo and Ivy is a small-batch American whiskey blend produced in Virginia. The name references the original owners of the land on which the distillery stands.

name: 12 Year
tableside: George Dickel is the other Tennessee whiskey — not Jack Daniel's. Bottled in Bond, 12 years old, $15. Dickel uses a cold charcoal mellowing process unique to their production and consistently produces whiskey that outperforms its price and recognition level.
story: George Dickel is Tennessee's second major whiskey producer , often overshadowed by Jack Daniel's but producing whiskey of comparable or superior quality at a fraction of the recognition. Like Jack Daniel's, Dickel uses the Lincoln County Process , filtering new whiskey through sugar maple charcoal before barreling , but does so while the whiskey is cold rather than warm, which Dickel believes produces a smoother result. The 12 Year Bottled-in-Bond is the most serious expression in their standard lineup: 100 proof, single season, government bonded, and twelve years in Tennessee oak.
trivia: George Dickel intentionally spells 'whisky' without the 'e' — the Scottish spelling — as a tribute to founder George Dickel's belief that his Tennessee whisky was comparable to fine Scotch.
The Lincoln County Process — filtering through charcoal before aging — is what legally distinguishes Tennessee whiskey from bourbon, though the two styles are otherwise produced similarly.

George Dickel's cold charcoal mellowing process runs the new spirit through charcoal at temperatures near freezing, which the distillery claims produces a distinctly smoother result than Jack Daniel's warmer process.
mash_bill: 84% corn, 8% rye, 8% malted barley
why: George Dickel is one of the few major Tennessee whiskey producers outside of Jack Daniel's. The 12 Year uses the Lincoln County Process , charcoal mellowing , and is aged significantly longer than most Tennessee whiskeys.

name: 15 Year
tableside: Fifteen years of Tennessee aging in a single barrel at 50.4% — this is George Dickel showing what patience produces. More oak and depth than the 12 Year, and the single barrel designation means every bottle reflects one specific cask. Still underpriced relative to comparable Kentucky bourbon.
story: George Dickel 15 Year Single Barrel represents the upper tier of the distillery's aging program , fifteen years of Tennessee climate aging in a single oak cask, bottled without blending or significant dilution. Tennessee's heat and humidity accelerate aging in ways similar to Kentucky, producing spirits with real wood complexity at relatively accessible age statements. The single barrel designation means every pour reflects one specific warehouse location, one specific barrel's interaction with the Tennessee environment over a decade and a half.
trivia: Single barrel Tennessee whiskey is relatively rare compared to the Kentucky bourbon market — most Tennessee producers release blended expressions, making Dickel's single barrel program distinctive.
Fifteen years in Tennessee's climate produces significant evaporation — the angel's share — which concentrates the remaining whiskey and increases proof naturally before bottling.

George Dickel's distillery in Cascade Hollow, Tennessee was closed between 1999 and 2003, meaning there is a gap in their aging inventory that makes expressions like the 15 Year particularly valuable.
mash_bill: 84% corn, 8% rye, 8% malted barley
why: George Dickel 15 Year is aged longer than any other Dickel standard expression. The extended time in Tennessee produces a notably more complex, wood-forward whiskey than the 12 Year.

name: 17 Year
tableside: Seventeen years of Tennessee whiskey development at barrel proof — among the oldest and rarest George Dickel expressions available. At $63 it's the most serious Dickel on the list and deserves to be in the conversation with Kentucky bourbons twice its price.
story: George Dickel 17 Year Barrel Proof is among the rarest expressions in the distillery's portfolio , nearly two decades of Tennessee aging at cask strength, released in very limited quantities. The extended aging pushes the oak influence significantly further than the 15 Year, developing deep caramel, dried fruit, and chocolate complexity that places it in the company of prestigious Kentucky bourbons with similar age statements. The barrel proof bottling preserves every compound that developed during those seventeen years without the dilution that would soften the edges.
trivia: George Dickel 17 Year was released as part of a limited series of older expressions from inventory accumulated before the distillery's 1999–2003 closure — making these bottles genuinely non-renewable once the stock is exhausted.

At 47% ABV, the 17 Year is lower proof than many barrel proof expressions — a reflection of Tennessee's climate and the natural evaporation that occurs over seventeen years, concentrating some compounds while reducing overall volume.

George Dickel has received significantly less collector attention than Kentucky bourbon producers despite producing whiskey of comparable quality — creating one of the better value opportunities in aged American whiskey.
mash_bill: 84% corn, 8% rye, 8% malted barley
why: The rarest standard Dickel expression, aged 17 years. Most Tennessee whiskey is released well under 10 years old , the 17 Year is a genuine outlier in the category.

tableside: Gentleman Jack goes through the Lincoln County charcoal mellowing process twice — before and after aging — which produces the smoothest Jack Daniel's expression. For a guest who wants something easy and familiar without committing to the full bar list.
story: Gentleman Jack is Jack Daniel's premium expression, distinguished from the standard Old No. 7 by a double charcoal mellowing process. The Lincoln County Process filters new spirit through sugar maple charcoal before barrel entry as standard practice at the distillery. For Gentleman Jack, that process is repeated a second time after aging, producing a noticeably smoother and more refined version of the Tennessee whiskey character. Jack Daniel's has been producing whiskey in Lynchburg, Tennessee since 1875, and Gentleman Jack was introduced in 1988 as the brand's first new expression in over a century.
trivia: Jack Daniel's Lincoln County Process filters new spirit through ten feet of sugar maple charcoal — Gentleman Jack adds a second filtration after aging, which removes additional congeners and softens the final spirit.

Jack Daniel's is technically not bourbon under Tennessee law, despite meeting all federal bourbon requirements, because the Lincoln County Process is considered an additional step that differentiates it from Kentucky-style production.
The Jack Daniel's distillery in Lynchburg, Tennessee is in a dry county — Moore County prohibits alcohol sales — making it one of the more ironic tourism destinations in American whiskey country.
mash_bill: 80% corn, 8% rye, 12% malted barley (Jack Daniel's charcoal mellowed)
why: Gentleman Jack is Jack Daniel's double-mellowed expression , charcoal filtered before and after barrel aging, rather than just before as is standard. The result is noticeably smoother than the standard Old No. 7.

name: Heritage Collection 2025
tableside: This is straight wheat whiskey — not a wheat-forward bourbon, but a whiskey where wheat is the primary grain, not corn. Heaven Hill's Heritage Collection is their annual prestige release and this is one of the rarest styles in American whiskey. A genuine find.
story: Heaven Hill Heritage Collection 2025 is a straight wheat whiskey , a category where wheat is the majority grain in the mashbill rather than corn, making it legally distinct from bourbon and producing a distinctly different flavor profile. Wheat whiskey is extraordinarily rare in American production, with very few distilleries producing it at all, and even fewer aging it to the level of complexity that Heaven Hill's deep inventory makes possible. For guests who love the soft, sweet character of wheated bourbons like Weller or Maker's Mark, straight wheat whiskey represents the logical extreme of that style , all wheat, all the time.
trivia: Straight wheat whiskey must contain at least 51% wheat in the mashbill — compared to bourbon's 51% corn requirement — and be aged in new charred oak, just like bourbon.
Heaven Hill's ability to release a prestige wheat whiskey reflects their extraordinary aging inventory, which includes barrels of rare grain combinations laid down across decades of production.

The Heritage Collection was established to honor Parker Beam's legacy and raises funds for ALS research — making the 2025 wheat whiskey both a rare spirit purchase and a contribution to a meaningful cause.
mash_bill: 78% corn, 10% rye, 12% malted barley (Heaven Hill)
why: Heaven Hill's annual Heritage Collection is one of the distillery's most limited and highly regarded releases. The 2025 edition is a specific barrel selection chosen to showcase a particular aspect of Heaven Hill's aging program.

name: Bourye
tableside: Bourye is exactly what it sounds like — a blend of bourbon and rye. High West combines both styles to create something that sits between them: more sweetness than straight rye, more spice than standard bourbon. A useful recommendation for guests who can't decide between the two.
story: High West Bourye is a blended American whiskey combining straight bourbon and straight rye , a style that predates modern category distinctions and was common in the nineteenth century when distillers blended freely across grain types. The result sits in an interesting middle ground: the caramel and vanilla sweetness of bourbon balanced by the dry spice and grain character of rye, producing a whiskey that appeals to fans of both categories while being fully characterized by neither. For guests who are drawn to both styles, Bourye provides a single glass answer.
trivia: The practice of blending bourbon and rye was common in American whiskey production before Prohibition, when category distinctions were less rigid and distillers experimented freely.
High West's blending philosophy is inspired by the Scottish tradition of blending different whisky styles to achieve complexity beyond what any single component can produce.
Bourye is made from several different aged bourbons and ryes sourced from multiple distilleries — the exact blend composition changes slightly based on barrel availability each year.
mash_bill: Blend: 75% corn/21% rye/4% barley (MGP bourbon) + 95% rye/5% barley (MGP rye)
why: High West Bourye blends aged bourbon and rye whiskeys from multiple distilleries, creating a category-defying expression. The name combines the two styles. Each annual release uses a different combination of barrels.

name: Campfire
detail: Utah · 46% · Blend of Scotch, Bourbon & Rye
tableside: Campfire blends three styles — Scotch, bourbon, and rye — into one glass. The peated Scotch adds smoke, the bourbon adds sweetness, and the rye adds spice. It shouldn't work as well as it does. Great for a guest who loves all three styles and wants to taste them together.
story: High West Campfire is one of the most conceptually ambitious blends in American whiskey , combining straight bourbon, straight rye, and blended malt Scotch whisky into a single expression. The idea is to evoke a campfire in the American West: smoke from the Scotch, warmth from the bourbon, spice from the rye, all coming together around an imagined fire in the Utah mountains. The fact that it works as well as it does is a testament to High West's blending skills , the three components complement each other in a way that feels natural rather than forced.
trivia: Campfire is legally classified as a 'blended American whiskey' because it contains Scotch — a foreign whisky — in addition to American straight whiskeys, preventing it from carrying a more specific American designation.

High West imports the peated Scotch component of Campfire from Scotland specifically for this blend — the distillery evaluates multiple Scotch expressions before selecting the one with the right smoke level to complement without dominating.
The concept of blending Scotch with American whiskey was nearly unheard of before High West introduced Campfire — it has since inspired other producers to explore cross-national blending.
mash_bill: Blend: bourbon, rye, and Scotch malt whisky from multiple sources
why: High West Campfire is one of the only American whiskeys to blend bourbon, rye, and Scotch malt whisky in a single bottle. The result is genuinely unlike any other whiskey on the list.

tableside: Jack Daniel's Old No. 7 is the world's best-selling American whiskey. It needs no introduction. At $9 it's the reliable familiar pour for any guest who wants something recognizable and easy.
story: Jack Daniel's Old No. 7 Tennessee Whiskey is the best-selling American whiskey in the world , over 13 million cases sold annually across more than 170 countries. Produced at the distillery Jasper Newton 'Jack' Daniel established in Lynchburg, Tennessee in 1875, it uses the Lincoln County Process , filtering new spirit through sugar maple charcoal , to produce the smooth, slightly sweet character that has made it globally ubiquitous. The distillery survived Prohibition, two World Wars, and the rise of vodka to become the defining symbol of American whiskey internationally.
trivia: Jack Daniel died in 1911 from a blood infection that began in his toe — reportedly caused by kicking his safe in frustration when he couldn't remember the combination early one morning.

The 'Old No. 7' designation's origin is disputed — various theories include a reference to a federal distillery registration number, a lucky number, or a girlfriend — the distillery maintains it doesn't know for certain.
Jack Daniel's is produced in Moore County, Tennessee — a dry county that prohibits alcohol sales — meaning the world's most famous whiskey is made in a place where you can't legally buy a drink.
mash_bill: 80% corn, 8% rye, 12% malted barley (charcoal mellowed, Jack Daniel's)
why: Jack Daniel's is America's best-selling whiskey, technically classified as Tennessee whiskey rather than bourbon due to the Lincoln County Process , charcoal mellowing before barrel entry.

name: Single Barrel
tableside: Jack Daniel's at full barrel strength — 64.05% with no dilution, no charcoal mellowing bypass. This is what Old No. 7 tastes like before water brings it down to 40%. A genuinely revelatory pour for anyone who thinks they know Jack Daniel's.
story: Jack Daniel's Single Barrel Barrel Proof is the most intense expression the distillery produces, one barrel at full cask strength without the significant dilution that brings standard Jack Daniel's down to 40% ABV. The single barrels are selected from the highest reaches of Warehouse D, where extreme temperature fluctuation during Kentucky summers and winters accelerates aging and concentrates flavor. At over 64%, it reveals the full character of the Tennessee distillate that the familiar bottle obscures: bold caramel and toasted oak with a weight and intensity that bears little resemblance to the everyday expression. For guests who underestimate Jack Daniel's, this is the corrective pour.
trivia: Jack Daniel's Single Barrel expressions are selected from the highest floors of the distillery's barrel houses, where the most dramatic temperature swings produce the boldest, most concentrated whiskey.

The significant proof difference between standard Jack Daniel's (40%) and the Barrel Proof (64.05%) reflects how much water is added during normal production — the barrel proof version shows the undiluted Tennessee distillate.

Jack Daniel's Single Barrel program allows private barrel selection for individuals and accounts — the distillery's 'barrel selector' program has produced thousands of unique single barrel releases for restaurants, retailers, and private buyers.
mash_bill: 80% corn, 8% rye, 12% malted barley (charcoal mellowed, Jack Daniel's)
why: The Jack Daniel's Single Barrel is selected from the highest reaches of Warehouse D, where temperature fluctuation produces the most developed flavor. Each barrel is bottled individually at 94 proof.

name: Batch #3 18 Year
tableside: Jacob's Pardon is American Light Whiskey — a legal category distinct from bourbon that allows aging in used or uncharred barrels. At 18 years and 71.35%, this is the highest proof spirit on the list. An extraordinarily rare category and pour.
story: Jacob's Pardon is an American Light Whiskey , a federally recognized but extremely rare category that allows aging in previously used or uncharred new oak barrels, rather than the new charred oak required for bourbon. The lighter barrel influence produces a spirit where the grain character is more prominent and the wood contribution is different in kind than standard bourbon aging. At 18 years old and 71.35% ABV, it is the oldest and highest-proof spirit on the entire bar list , a genuinely unusual and historically obscure category made real in a single extraordinary bottle.
trivia: American Light Whiskey was created as a legal category in 1972, partly to allow American distillers to compete with the lighter Scotch and Canadian whiskies that were gaining market share at the time.
The category has remained extremely obscure — very few producers have made significant quantities of American Light Whiskey, and aged examples like Jacob's Pardon 18 Year are extraordinarily rare.
At 71.35% ABV, Jacob's Pardon Batch #3 is the highest-proof spirit on the list — a proof level that requires careful handling and small pours, with water almost obligatory for safe enjoyment.
mash_bill: Undisclosed — 18 year sourced blend, multiple distilleries
why: Jacob's Pardon is a very small production American whiskey brand. The Batch 3 expression is 18 years old , exceptional age for an American whiskey outside of the major allocated categories.

name: Unblended American
tableside: Michter's Unblended American is a genuine curiosity — a whiskey that uses multiple grain types but doesn't fit neatly into any standard category. Light, approachable, and interesting. A good recommendation when a guest wants something different without going too far.
story: Michter's US*1 Unblended American Whiskey is a single barrel expression that uses a grain recipe falling outside the legal definitions of bourbon, rye, or wheat whiskey, categorized simply as American Whiskey by the federal government. Michter's was originally founded in Pennsylvania in 1753 and is one of America's oldest whiskey brands, though the modern operation in Louisville is a revival. The unblended designation means every bottle comes from a single cask rather than a batch blend, adding complete provenance to an already unusual product. It represents Michter's philosophy of making interesting whiskey regardless of whether it fits an established category.
trivia: The 'American Whiskey' designation on Michter's Unblended American reflects a mashbill and production process that doesn't meet the specific requirements for bourbon, rye, wheat, or other defined American whiskey categories.
Michter's US*1 Unblended American is bottled from individual barrels rather than batches — each release reflects one specific barrel's development, creating natural variation between releases.

The American Whiskey category is one of the broadest in federal spirits law — it simply requires that the spirit be distilled from a grain mash in the United States and aged in oak, without the specific constraints that define bourbon or rye.
mash_bill: Undisclosed American whiskey — not classified as bourbon or rye
why: Michter's Unblended American Whiskey is made from a mash that doesn't qualify as bourbon or rye under federal standards. It is an exploration of what American whiskey tastes like outside of legal category requirements.

name: Smokehouse
tableside: Murlarkey Smokehouse finishes in staves smoked with pork belly — yes, actually. It's a Virginia distillery doing something genuinely no one else is doing. The smoke is real, the savory quality is subtle but present, and it's a great conversation piece at $17.
story: Murlarkey Distillery in Bristow, Virginia produces Smokehouse using a finishing technique that is entirely unique in American whiskey , oak staves that have been infused with pork belly smoke are placed in the aging barrel, imparting a subtle savory, smoky character alongside the traditional wood influence. The result is a whiskey that sits at the intersection of Virginia's barbecue culture and its emerging craft spirits scene. It is unquestionably a novelty, but a novelty executed with genuine craft and a flavor profile that is more interesting and subtle than it sounds on paper.
trivia: Murlarkey Distillery is located in Prince William County, Virginia — part of the growing Virginia craft spirits corridor that has developed around the state's permissive distilling regulations.
The pork belly smoke infusion technique used for Smokehouse is proprietary to Murlarkey — no other commercial distillery has used animal fat-smoked wood as a finishing element in American whiskey.
Virginia is one of the fastest-growing craft distilling states in the country, with over 100 licensed distilleries operating as of 2024 — a dramatic increase from fewer than ten a decade ago.
mash_bill: Undisclosed — American whiskey with smoked elements
why: Murlarkey is a Virginia distillery that specializes in smoked expressions. The Smokehouse whiskey uses a smoked grain in the mash rather than peated malt, producing a different character from Scottish-style peated whisky.

name: Wheated 7 Year 'The Beast'
detail: Colorado · 57.15% · Single Barrel · Barrel Proof · VA Select
tableside: Old Elk 'The Beast' — a wheated bourbon at full barrel proof, seven years old, Virginia select. Old Elk is a Colorado distillery that has built a strong reputation for wheat-forward expressions. At 57.15% this has real intensity while keeping the wheat's natural softness.
story: Old Elk Distillery is located in Fort Collins, Colorado , a state not historically associated with serious whiskey production but increasingly home to ambitious craft producers. 'The Beast' is their wheated bourbon at full barrel proof, a name that reflects both the proof level and the intensity of the expression. Seven years of Colorado aging, where the altitude and climate create different aging conditions than Kentucky, produces a wheated bourbon that is genuinely distinctive. The Virginia ABC select barrel program chose this specific barrel from Old Elk's inventory for the Virginia market.
trivia: Colorado's high altitude affects distillation in measurable ways — lower atmospheric pressure changes the boiling point of alcohol and water, altering the distillation process and the congener profile of the final spirit.

Old Elk's master distiller, Greg Metze, spent 38 years at MGP Distillery in Indiana before moving to Colorado — bringing significant expertise in grain whiskey production to the craft distilling space.

Wheated bourbon aged at barrel proof is relatively uncommon — most wheated expressions are released at lower proof to emphasize the style's natural softness, making 'The Beast' an unusual combination of style and intensity.
mash_bill: Undisclosed wheated bourbon — Old Elk, Colorado
why: Old Elk's Wheated Bourbon 7 Year uses 45% soft red winter wheat , one of the highest wheat percentages of any bourbon. The Slow Cut proofing process takes weeks rather than hours, preserving flavor that rapid dilution would destroy.

name: Straight Wheat 7 Year
detail: Colorado · 57.15% · Single Barrel · Barrel Proof · VA Select
tableside: Where 'The Beast' is a wheated bourbon, this is a straight wheat whiskey — wheat as the majority grain, not just a flavoring agent. The difference is noticeable: more grain presence, less corn sweetness, a more direct expression of what the wheat itself tastes like.
story: Old Elk Straight Wheat Whiskey 7 Year is a direct companion to 'The Beast' wheated bourbon , same distillery, same barrel proof, same age, same Virginia select designation, but a fundamentally different grain bill. Where the wheated bourbon uses wheat as a secondary grain alongside corn, the straight wheat whiskey uses wheat as the majority grain, producing a more grain-forward spirit where the wheat character dominates without corn sweetness to soften it. Having both expressions available creates a genuinely educational tasting opportunity for guests interested in how grain choice shapes whiskey.
trivia: Straight wheat whiskey must contain at least 51% wheat in the mashbill and be aged in new charred oak — it is one of the rarer American whiskey categories, with far fewer producers than bourbon or rye.

The flavor difference between wheated bourbon and straight wheat whiskey is primarily about grain dominance — in wheated bourbon, corn provides the base sweetness while wheat softens; in straight wheat, wheat is the primary character driver.

Old Elk's decision to produce both wheated bourbon and straight wheat whiskey at comparable specifications allows for one of the clearest grain comparison opportunities available in American craft whiskey.
mash_bill: 100% wheat whiskey
why: Old Elk Straight Wheat Whiskey is made from 100% wheat , not classified as bourbon because corn is not the dominant grain. One of the only widely available wheat whiskeys in the American market.

name: 18th Edition
detail: Kentucky · 53.5% · 14 Year · Malt Whiskey · Cognac Finish
tableside: Parker's Heritage 18th Edition is a Kentucky malt whiskey — made from malted barley like Scotch, but produced in Kentucky with bourbon-style aging. Finished in Cognac barrels. The combination of American malt character and French brandy cask produces something genuinely unlike anything else on the list.
story: Parker's Heritage 18th Edition explores American malt whiskey , a category that uses malted barley as the majority grain, like Scotch single malt, but is produced and aged in the American bourbon tradition. After 14 years in new charred oak, the whiskey was finished in Cognac barrels, adding the dried fruit and brandy complexity of French spirits production to an already unusual American base. The result is a whiskey that sits at the intersection of multiple traditions , Scotch malt production methods, Kentucky aging, and European wine-based spirit finishing , united by Heaven Hill's commitment to the Parker Beam legacy.
trivia: American malt whiskey — using malted barley as the majority grain — is legally recognized but extremely rare in commercial production, as most American distillers focus on corn and rye-based mashbills.

Cognac barrels come from France's Charente region, where they held grape brandy for years before being emptied and repurposed for whiskey finishing — the wine and brandy residue contributes dried fruit and grape spirit character.
The 18th Edition of Parker's Heritage represents 18 consecutive years of annual limited releases honoring Parker Beam — each edition explores a different aspect of American whiskey production.
mash_bill: Undisclosed — Heaven Hill limited Heritage release
why: Parker's Heritage 18th Edition is Heaven Hill's annual limited collector's release, chosen personally by the Parker family. Each year's expression is a fundamentally different whiskey.

name: 19th Edition
detail: Kentucky · 61.25% · 11 Year · Straight Whiskey · Barrel Proof
tableside: The 19th Edition is an 11-year straight whiskey at full barrel proof — Heaven Hill's most intense annual prestige release. At 61.25% it has serious heat, but the age gives it the structure to handle it. A meaningful pour in honor of Parker Beam.
story: Parker's Heritage 19th Edition is an 11-year straight American whiskey released at full barrel proof, the most powerful release in the series to date at 61.25%. Without the softening effect of a cask finish or dilution, the full character of Heaven Hill's distillate at 11 years of age is presented without compromise. The Parker's Heritage Collection was established to honor Parker Beam, who served as Heaven Hill's master distiller for over 30 years before being diagnosed with ALS in 2012. A portion of every bottle sold contributes to ALS research in his memory, making it both one of the most powerful and most meaningful releases on the list.
trivia: The 19th consecutive annual release of Parker's Heritage marks nearly two decades of Heaven Hill honoring Parker Beam — a sustained commitment that has raised significant funds for ALS research.

At 61.25% ABV, the 19th Edition is among the highest-proof expressions in the Parker's Heritage series — a deliberate choice to showcase the full intensity of Heaven Hill's distillate without dilution.

Parker Beam was known throughout the industry for his preference for high-proof, uncompromising whiskey — releasing barrel proof expressions in his honor reflects both his personal taste and his distilling philosophy.
mash_bill: Undisclosed — Heaven Hill limited Heritage release
why: The 19th annual Parker's Heritage Collection release. Heaven Hill releases one expression per year in this series and never produces the same whiskey twice.

detail: Tennessee · 60.15% · Single Barrel · Barrel Proof · VA Select
tableside: Uncle Nearest honors Nathan 'Nearest' Green — the formerly enslaved man who taught Jack Daniel to distill and whose contribution to Tennessee whiskey was erased from history for over a century. At barrel proof and $30, it's both a great whiskey and an important story.
story: Uncle Nearest Premium Whiskey was founded in 2017 to honor Nathan 'Nearest' Green, a formerly enslaved man in Lincoln County, Tennessee who is documented as the first known African-American master distiller in US history. Historical records and interviews with Jack Daniel's descendants confirm that Green taught Daniel the art of whiskey making, yet his name was absent from the official history of American whiskey for over 150 years. Fawn Weaver founded Uncle Nearest to restore his legacy, producing Tennessee whiskey that carries his name and contributes to scholarships for his descendants. The whiskey itself has won numerous awards and stands entirely on its own merits.
trivia: Nathan 'Nearest' Green is documented in historical records as teaching Jack Daniel to distill — Jack Daniel's own great-great-nephew confirmed this in interviews that informed Fawn Weaver's research.
Uncle Nearest is the fastest-growing American whiskey brand in history, reaching sales milestones in years that took other brands decades — driven by both the story and the quality of the whiskey.
The Uncle Nearest Premium Whiskey Foundation provides scholarships to descendants of Nearest Green — connecting the brand's commercial success directly to the education of the man's family.
mash_bill: Undisclosed — Tennessee whiskey, Lincoln County process
why: Uncle Nearest is named after Nathan 'Nearest' Green, the first known African American master distiller, who taught Jack Daniel the Lincoln County Process in the 1850s. One of the most historically significant American whiskey brands.

tableside: Crown Royal is the benchmark Canadian whisky — smooth, light, and approachable. A reliable pour for a guest who wants something easy and familiar without committing to the complexity of the bourbon or rye list.
story: Crown Royal was created in 1939 specifically as a gift for King George VI and Queen Elizabeth during their royal tour of Canada , the first visit of a reigning British monarch to the country. Samuel Bronfman of Seagram's blended 600 different whiskies to create a spirit worthy of royalty, and the distinctive purple bag and crown-shaped bottle have remained unchanged since. Canadian whisky law allows blending of grain spirits with small amounts of other spirits, and the smoothness that defines Crown Royal comes from this flexibility , corn whisky blended with rye whisky for just enough spice to be interesting.
trivia: Crown Royal was created specifically for the 1939 royal visit — Samuel Bronfman reportedly taste-tested hundreds of blends before settling on the one he considered worthy of the King and Queen.

Canadian whisky law allows up to 9.09% of the blend to consist of other spirits — including bourbon, sherry, or wine — which gives Canadian blenders significant flexibility that American distillers don't have.

The purple velvet bag that wraps each Crown Royal bottle was originally part of the royal presentation and has become one of the most recognizable packaging elements in spirits — many consumers keep and repurpose the bags.
mash_bill: Blended Canadian whisky — proprietary blend of corn and rye whiskies (Crown Royal)
why: Canada's best-selling whisky, produced at Crown Royal's Gimli Distillery in Manitoba. A blend of over 50 whiskies, chosen for balance and consistency. The signature purple bag is one of the most recognizable packaging choices in spirits.

name: 12 Year
tableside: Chivas Regal 12 Year is the classic blended Scotch — smooth, honey-forward, and broadly approachable. For a guest who wants Scotch without the smoke or intensity of Islay, blended Scotch like Chivas is the right direction.
story: Chivas Regal was created by the Chivas Brothers in Aberdeen, Scotland, and became one of the defining blended Scotch whiskies of the twentieth century , particularly in America, where it was positioned as the premium Scotch for sophisticated drinkers during the postwar era. The 12 Year blend combines single malt and grain whiskies from across Scotland, unified by the smooth, honey-forward character that has made Chivas one of the world's best-selling Scotch brands. It was one of the first whiskies to prominently feature an age statement as a quality guarantee.
trivia: Chivas Regal was one of the first blended Scotch whiskies to carry a guaranteed age statement — the '12 Year' designation means every whisky in the blend has been aged for at least 12 years.
The Chivas Brothers company began as a grocery and provisions store in Aberdeen in the 1800s before transitioning to whisky production and blending.
Chivas Regal became closely associated with American luxury consumption in the 1950s and 60s — it was the Scotch of choice in advertising and popular culture as a symbol of arrived sophistication.
mash_bill: Blended Scotch whisky — grain and malt whiskies (Chivas Regal)
why: Chivas Regal 12 Year is one of Scotland's most recognizable blended Scotch whiskies, consistently produced since 1909. Blended from over 20 malt and grain whiskies aged a minimum of 12 years.

name: 12 Year
tableside: Dewar's 12 Year uses a double aging process — the blend is returned to casks after initial blending for additional maturation, which produces unusual smoothness and integration. A step above standard blended Scotch at a fair price.
story: Dewar's was founded by John Dewar in Perth, Scotland in 1846, and his sons Tommy and John Alexander Dewar were responsible for turning it into one of the first globally recognized Scotch brands in the late nineteenth century. Tommy Dewar famously circled the world promoting the whisky and became one of the most colorful figures in spirits marketing history. The 12 Year uses Dewar's proprietary double maturation process, where blended whiskies are returned to casks for additional aging together after assembly. This secondary maturation allows the components to integrate more fully, producing a smoother and more cohesive result than standard blended Scotch at this price.
trivia: Dewar's double maturation process — aging the blend a second time after initial blending — was developed by master blender Tom Aitken and is now a defining characteristic of the brand's production method.

John Dewar founded his whisky business in Perth, Scotland in 1846 — his sons Tommy and John Jr. expanded the brand internationally, with Tommy Dewar becoming one of the most celebrated whisky ambassadors of his era.
Dewar's is now owned by Bacardi, which has invested significantly in the double maturation program and the brand's premium positioning since acquiring it in 1998.
mash_bill: Blended Scotch whisky — grain and malt whiskies (Dewar's)
why: Dewar's 12 Year uses John Dewar's double aging process , the blend is assembled and then returned to casks for additional maturation. One of the few blended Scotch producers to use this technique as standard practice.

name: Black
tableside: Johnnie Walker Black is the world's best-selling blended Scotch — 12 years old, lightly smoky, and consistently well-made. The go-to recommendation for a guest who wants a recognizable blended Scotch with genuine quality behind the label.
story: Johnnie Walker Black Label was created in 1909 as a step up from the original Red Label, using whiskies aged a minimum of 12 years including contributions from Islay distilleries that add the light smoke that distinguishes it from other standard blended Scotch expressions. It is the world's best-selling blended Scotch and one of the most recognizable spirits brands globally , a status earned over more than a century of consistent quality rather than marketing alone. The square bottle and diagonal label that define the Johnnie Walker look were innovations designed to allow more bottles per shipping crate.
trivia: Johnnie Walker Black Label's signature light smoke comes from Islay single malts blended into the base — typically around 40 whiskies from across Scotland are used in each batch.
The iconic diagonal label on Johnnie Walker bottles was a practical innovation — tilting the label 24 degrees allows more text to be read while the bottle is at a natural viewing angle.
John Walker, a grocer in Kilmarnock, Scotland, began blending whiskies in the 1820s to create consistency for his customers — his family continued refining the blends for generations after his death.
mash_bill: Blended Scotch whisky — grain and malt whiskies (Johnnie Walker)
why: Johnnie Walker Black Label is one of the world's best-selling Scotch whiskies. A blend of over 40 malt and grain whiskies aged a minimum of 12 years, with Islay malts providing the signature smoky undertone.

name: Green
tableside: Johnnie Walker Green is a blended malt — no grain whisky, only single malts from four different distilleries blended together. Fifteen years old. It's the most complex and interesting Johnnie Walker expression and significantly underappreciated relative to the color above it.
story: Johnnie Walker Green Label is a blended malt Scotch whisky, a category that combines multiple single malts without the addition of grain whisky, producing more complexity and character than standard blended Scotch. The four single malts, from Talisker on Skye, Linkwood in Speyside, Cragganmore in Speyside, and Caol Ila on Islay, represent different Scottish regions and flavor traditions. Smoke comes from Talisker and Caol Ila, fruit and floral character from Linkwood, and spice from Cragganmore. At 15 years old it is the most serious standard Johnnie Walker expression and one of the better values in aged blended malt Scotch on any list.
trivia: Blended malt Scotch (formerly called 'vatted malt') uses only single malt whiskies from multiple distilleries — no grain whisky — producing a more complex and characterful result than standard blended Scotch.
The four malts in Johnnie Walker Green — Talisker, Linkwood, Cragganmore, and Caol Ila — represent Highland, Speyside, and Islay regional characters, creating genuine breadth in a single glass.

Green Label was discontinued in 2012 due to declining sales before being relaunched in 2016 following strong demand from whisky enthusiasts who recognized its quality — one of the more successful product revivals in Scotch history.
mash_bill: Blended malt Scotch whisky — 100% malt whiskies, no grain (Johnnie Walker Green)
why: Johnnie Walker Green Label is a blended malt , 100% malt whiskies from four distilleries with no grain whisky component. One of the few widely available blended malts, and a significantly more complex drink than a standard blended Scotch.

name: Cidre Blanc
tableside: ANXO is DC's premier cidery — this is as far from supermarket cider as you can get. Bone dry, clean, and closer to a dry white wine than anything sweet. Great for guests who think they don't like cider.
story: ANXO Cidery is Washington DC's first cidery, founded in 2015 and rooted in the traditional cider traditions of the Basque Country and Brittany rather than the sweet American cider market. The Cidre Blanc is made from a blend of bittersweet and bittersharp apples , varieties grown specifically for cider production rather than eating , which produce the dry, tannic character that defines traditional European cider. The result drinks more like a natural white wine than anything you'd find in a supermarket, and it's a genuine point of DC pride.
trivia: ANXO takes its name from a Basque word meaning 'angel' — a nod to the Basque Country's deep cider-making tradition, where cider houses called sagardotegiak have operated for centuries.
Traditional cider apples are high in tannins and acidity but low in sweetness — they're essentially inedible raw but produce complex, dry fermented cider that table apples can't match.
DC has a surprisingly strong cider culture for an urban market — ANXO has helped establish the city as a serious destination for craft cider in a way that few American cities can claim.

name: German Style Pilsner
detail: Fort Bragg, CA · 4.7% · German Style Pilsner
tableside: A German-style pilsner brewed on the Northern California coast by North Coast Brewing. Crisp, clean, and dry with a gentle floral hop character. The go-to recommendation for a guest who just wants a cold beer without much complexity.
story: Scrimshaw Pilsner is brewed by North Coast Brewing Company in Fort Bragg, California — a coastal town on the Mendocino coast whose fishing and maritime heritage inspired the name. Scrimshaw refers to the engraving art practiced by sailors and whalers on whale bone and ivory. North Coast is one of the pioneering craft breweries of the American craft beer movement, founded in 1988, and Scrimshaw is their take on the German Pilsner tradition: light, dry, and built for drinking rather than contemplation.
trivia: The German Pilsner style originated in the 19th century as a cleaner, drier evolution of the Czech Pilsner developed in Plzeň in 1842. Where Czech Pilsner is rounder and maltier, German Pils is crisper, drier, and more hop-forward — particularly in the floral, herbal Hallertau hop character.

Scrimshaw — the art of engraving detailed scenes on whale bone, ivory, or shells — was practiced by sailors during long voyages as a way to pass time. The tradition is most closely associated with American whalers of the 18th and 19th centuries, particularly those from New England ports.
North Coast Brewing in Fort Bragg is also known for Brother Thelonious Belgian Abbey Ale and Old Rasputin Russian Imperial Stout, both considered American craft benchmarks in their respective styles.

name: Kellerbier
tableside: Weihenstephaner is the world's oldest brewery — over a thousand years old. Their Kellerbier is unfiltered and unpasteurized, served naturally cloudy straight from the tank. Fresher and crisper than the Hefeweizen, with a soft malt character and a gentle hop presence. The style that Munich beer halls have been pouring for centuries.
story: Weihenstephan Brewery in Freising, Bavaria holds a continuous brewing record dating to 1040 AD — over a thousand years of uninterrupted production on the same site, making it the oldest brewery in the world by a significant margin. Kellerbier — literally cellar beer — is an unfiltered, unpasteurized lager style traditionally served directly from the lagering cellar. The style predates modern filtration and represents how Bavarian lager was consumed before industrial brewing methods standardized the clear, shelf-stable product most people associate with German beer today.
trivia: Weihenstephan's brewing history is documented to 1040 AD, when Benedictine monks at the monastery on the hill above Freising received a license to brew and sell beer — making it the oldest brewery in the world with a documented continuous history.

Kellerbier translates directly to cellar beer — the name refers to its origin in the lagering cellars where Bavarian brewers stored beer at cold temperatures to condition it. Serving it unfiltered and unpasteurized directly from the cellar was standard practice before refrigerated distribution made shelf-stable filtered lager the norm.

Weihenstephan is now owned by the State of Bavaria and operates as part of the Technical University of Munich's brewing and food technology program — making it simultaneously the world's oldest brewery and an active academic research institution.

name: XX Bitter
tableside: De Ranke XX Bitter is one of Belgium's most celebrated hoppy ales — unusually bitter for a Belgian beer, which traditionally lean malty and sweet. Floral, herbal, and intensely dry. For guests who love IPAs but want something more nuanced and complex.
story: De Ranke is a small Belgian brewery in Dottignies, Hainaut, founded by Nino Bacelle and Guido Devos in 1994. The XX Bitter was their first and defining beer , a deliberate reaction against the sweetness that dominated Belgian ale production at the time. Using Hallertau and Brewer's Gold hops in unusually generous quantities for the Belgian style, it produces a beer of remarkable bitterness and floral complexity that has influenced a generation of European craft brewers. It is considered one of the finest Belgian pale ales in existence.
trivia: De Ranke uses whole flower hops rather than pellets in their brewing process — a traditional approach that produces a more complex and nuanced bitterness than compressed hop pellets.
The 'XX' in the name references the double hopping rate used in production — significantly more hops than a standard Belgian pale ale recipe would call for.

De Ranke's small production volumes and commitment to traditional methods have made them a cult brewery among Belgian beer enthusiasts — their beers are often unavailable outside of specialty accounts and import channels.

name: Big Tomorrow IPA
tableside: Right Proper is one of DC's best breweries — Big Tomorrow is their flagship West Coast IPA, brewed with Centennial, Cashmere, and Cryo-Mosaic hops. Grapefruit, lime zest, pine, and a clean bitter finish. 7.2% with real hop presence. The local IPA for guests who want something well-made and unfussy.
story: Right Proper Brewing Company opened in Shaw, Washington DC in 2013 and has become one of the most respected craft breweries in the Mid-Atlantic. The brewery is named after a DC colloquialism , 'right proper' meaning genuinely good, done correctly , which reflects their commitment to well-crafted, approachable beer rooted in British brewing tradition adapted for American tastes. Big Tomorrow is their IPA flagship: balanced, drinkable, and representative of the modern American IPA style without the aggressive bitterness that can alienate casual drinkers.
trivia: Right Proper's original location in the historic Blagden Alley neighborhood of Shaw, DC helped anchor the area's transformation into one of the city's most vibrant dining and drinking destinations.

Big Tomorrow is brewed with Cryo-Mosaic hops — a concentrated form of Mosaic that delivers intense hop aroma without adding extra vegetal bitterness, which is why the beer reads bright and clean rather than heavy.

The brewery takes inspiration from British pub culture — their taproom in Shaw is designed to feel like a neighborhood local rather than a destination craft beer bar, which reflects their philosophy of making beer that's part of daily life.

name: Sticky Rice Sour
detail: Leesburg, VA · 5% · Sour with Mango, Rice Milk & Toasted Coconut
tableside: This is the most adventurous beer on the list — a sour ale brewed with sticky rice, mango, rice milk, and toasted coconut. Tropical, tart, and unlike anything else. Perfect for a guest who says they don't drink beer, or for someone who wants something genuinely different.
story: Crooked Run Fermentation is a Leesburg, Virginia brewery known for creative, ingredient-forward sour ales that draw on global flavor traditions. The Sticky Rice Sour is their most celebrated expression of this philosophy: a kettle sour base brewed with actual sticky rice for texture and body, then conditioned on mango puree, rice milk, and toasted coconut. The result is a beer that tastes like a tropical dessert while maintaining the tartness and refreshment that makes sour beer genuinely thirst-quenching. It's a Virginia craft beer that has earned national attention.
trivia: Kettle sours like Crooked Run's Sticky Rice Sour are acidified before fermentation by introducing lactobacillus bacteria to the warm wort — a faster process than traditional barrel souring that produces a clean, consistent tartness.

Sticky rice (glutinous rice) contains different starch compounds than regular brewing rice — when used in beer production it adds a distinctive silky body and subtle sweetness that standard barley malt cannot replicate.

Crooked Run has become one of Virginia's most decorated craft breweries, winning awards at national competitions for their sour and mixed-fermentation programs — putting Leesburg on the map as a serious craft beer destination.

name: Trappist Tripel
tableside: Westmalle Tripel is the original Trappist Tripel — this style was literally invented here. Brewed by monks inside a monastery, 9.5% but deceptively smooth. One of the most complex and rewarding beers in the world. Warn guests about the ABV.
story: The Abbey of Our Lady of the Sacred Heart in Malle, Belgium is one of only a handful of Trappist monasteries in the world that brew beer commercially , and Westmalle is among the most revered of all. The Tripel style was literally invented at Westmalle in 1934, when the monks developed a stronger, golden version of their existing ales to serve as a more substantial drink. The recipe has barely changed since. Today Westmalle Tripel is considered the benchmark Trappist Tripel against which all others are measured , complex, spicy, and dangerously smooth for its 9.5% ABV.
trivia: To legally carry the 'Authentic Trappist Product' designation, a beer must be brewed within the walls of a Trappist monastery, under the supervision of monks, with profits going to the monastery or charitable causes — fewer than a dozen breweries worldwide qualify.

Westmalle first brewed this strong pale ale in 1934 to celebrate the opening of their new brewery hall — it was among the first golden strong ales of its kind, at a time when most Belgian abbey beers were dark. The recipe was refined and named Tripel in 1956 and has been essentially unchanged since.

The yeast strain used by Westmalle is proprietary and closely guarded — it produces the distinctive fruity and spicy ester profile that defines the Tripel style and has never been commercially released.

name: Noir de Dottignies
tableside: De Ranke's dark expression — the same small Belgian brewery as the XX Bitter, but here going deep and rich. Dark fruit, chocolate, roasted malt, 9% ABV. For a guest who loves big red wines or bourbon, this is the bridge beer.
story: Noir de Dottignies is De Ranke's dark strong ale , a completely different expression of the brewery's philosophy from the XX Bitter, but equally uncompromising. Where the XX Bitter uses aggressive hopping to achieve dryness and complexity, Noir de Dottignies achieves it through dark malt character: chocolate, roasted grain, and dried dark fruit at 9% ABV. It represents the full range of what De Ranke can do , from their lightest, hoppiest expressions to this deep, warming winter-style ale. Both are made in small quantities and both reward guests who take the time to explore them.
trivia: Dottignies, the small Belgian town where De Ranke is located, is in the Hainaut province — a region with a long history of farmhouse and abbey brewing that predates the modern craft beer movement by centuries.

Dark strong ales like Noir de Dottignies are sometimes called 'Belgian quads' informally, though the style has no strict legal definition — they typically range from 8–12% ABV with complex dark malt and yeast-driven fruit character.

De Ranke produces both Noir de Dottignies and the XX Bitter with the same core production team and facilities — the dramatic difference between the two beers illustrates how much of a beer's character comes from recipe and ingredient choice rather than equipment.

name: Rise Up Coffee Stout
tableside: Evolution brews this stout with Rise Up Coffee — a Maryland roaster — which gives it a genuine, fresh coffee character rather than the artificial coffee flavoring you get in most coffee beers. Rich, smooth, and approachable. Great dessert beer recommendation.
story: Evolution Craft Brewing in Salisbury, Maryland is one of the Eastern Shore's most celebrated breweries, known for their approachable but thoughtfully crafted ales and lagers. The Rise Up Coffee Stout is a collaboration with Rise Up Coffee Roasters, also from Maryland's Eastern Shore , a genuinely local partnership that uses freshly roasted coffee in the brewing process rather than coffee extract or flavoring. The result is a stout with real coffee complexity: not just bitter and dark but genuinely aromatic and nuanced, the way a well-made espresso is different from a cheap cup of coffee.
trivia: Rise Up Coffee Roasters is based in Easton, Maryland — the same Eastern Shore region as Evolution Brewing — making this a genuinely hyperlocal collaboration between two Maryland producers.

Coffee in brewing is typically added during the conditioning phase rather than the boil, which preserves the delicate aromatic compounds that heat would destroy — the same reason cold brew coffee tastes different from hot-brewed coffee.

Stouts brewed with local coffee have become a signature style for mid-Atlantic craft breweries, reflecting both the region's strong coffee culture and the growing emphasis on ingredient provenance in craft brewing.

name: Pissenlit
tableside: Fantôme is one of the most eccentric and celebrated saison breweries in the world — brewed in a tiny farmhouse in rural Belgium by essentially one person. Pissenlit means 'dandelion' in French and it's brewed with the plant. Wild, funky, earthy, and completely unlike anything else on the list. The 750mL is a sharing moment.
story: Brasserie Fantôme is operated almost entirely by Dany Prignon from a small farmhouse in Soy, a hamlet in the Ardennes region of Belgium. Fantôme produces some of the most eccentric and celebrated saisons in the world , each batch is slightly different, deliberately unpredictable, and deeply rooted in the farmhouse brewing tradition that originated in rural Wallonia centuries ago. Pissenlit (French for dandelion) is brewed with dandelion plants harvested locally, adding a wild, earthy bitterness that complements the funky Fantôme house yeast. It is a beer for guests who understand that not everything needs to be consistent , and who appreciate the romance of something made by hand in a Belgian farmhouse.
trivia: Fantôme's brewery in Soy, Belgium is genuinely tiny — Dany Prignon operates it largely alone, which means production volumes are very small and each batch reflects the conditions and ingredients available at that specific moment.

Dandelion was historically used as a bittering agent in beer before hops became the dominant flavoring — it produces a different kind of bitterness than hops, more earthy and vegetal than floral and resinous.

Saison originated as a farmhouse beer style in Wallonia, the French-speaking region of Belgium, brewed by farmers to sustain agricultural workers through the summer — the style traditionally uses whatever local ingredients are available, which is why Fantôme's use of dandelion is historically appropriate.

name: Suns Out Hops Out
tableside: Solace is a Northern Virginia brewery right down the road — Suns Out Hops Out is their flagship session IPA, brewed with Citra and Mosaic hops for citrus and tropical fruit character at a very sessionable 4.7%. The local easy-drinking option for guests who want a hop-forward beer without the weight.
story: Solace Brewing Company is based in Dulles, Virginia , about 20 miles from Trummer's , making it one of the most genuinely local beers on the list. Founded in 2016, Solace has built a strong regional following for their approachable, well-crafted ales and lagers that reflect the Northern Virginia beer landscape. Suns Out Hops Out is their flagship session IPA: brewed with Citra and Mosaic hops, two of the most aromatic hop varieties developed in the last two decades, producing the tropical fruit and citrus character that defines the modern American IPA style at a low enough ABV to drink comfortably through a meal.
trivia: Citra hops, used in Suns Out Hops Out, were developed by the Hop Breeding Company and released in 2007 — they have become one of the most widely used hops in American craft brewing for their intense citrus and tropical fruit aroma.

Mosaic hops, the other variety in this beer, produce a more complex aromatic profile than Citra — described as simultaneously fruity, earthy, and herbal, which adds depth to the citrus-forward Citra character.

Session IPAs became a defined style category around 2010, as craft beer drinkers sought the hop flavor of IPAs without the high alcohol content — Suns Out Hops Out at 4.7% is a textbook example of the style done well.

name: Classic Lite
tableside: Honor Brewing is a veteran-owned brewery in Sterling, Virginia — right in our backyard. Classic Lite is their take on a light lager done properly: 99 calories, clean and crisp, brewed with noble hops for just enough character. The right answer when a guest wants something light and easy.
story: Honor Brewing Company was founded in Sterling, Virginia in 2014 by Allen Cage with a mission explicitly tied to honoring military service and sacrifice , every beer in their lineup is named in tribute to those who have served, and the brewery maintains strong ties to the veteran community in Northern Virginia. Classic Lite is their interpretation of the American light lager , a style they describe as 'a micro approach to a macro style,' using Bohemian Pilsner malt and Hallertauer noble hops to produce something cleaner and more flavorful than the mass-market light beers it references. At 99 calories and 4.3% it's the most approachable beer on the list, and the veteran-owned local story makes it worth mentioning.
trivia: Honor Brewing's mission to honor military service is reflected in every aspect of their business — the brewery donates a portion of proceeds to veteran organizations and regularly hosts events for the military community in Northern Virginia.

Hallertauer hops, used in Classic Lite, are one of the oldest and most traditional noble hop varieties, originating in the Hallertau region of Bavaria — they produce a mild, floral bitterness that is the backbone of classic German lager styles.

Sterling, Virginia — where Honor Brewing is located — is home to a significant concentration of military and government employees given its proximity to Dulles Airport and several major federal facilities, making Honor's veteran-focused mission particularly resonant with the local community.

tableside: Montenegro is the gateway amaro — the lightest and most approachable on the list. Sweet orange, vanilla, floral notes, with just enough bitterness to be interesting. If a guest has never had amaro, this is where to start.
story: Amaro Montenegro is produced in Bologna, Italy and is one of the most beloved amaros in the country , Italians refer to it affectionately as 'the liqueur of virtues.' Created in 1885 by Stanislao Cobianchi, it is made from a blend of 40 botanicals including orange peel, vanilla, and various herbs and roots. Its relatively low bitterness and sweet, floral character make it the most accessible entry point into the amaro category , a category that, at its more intense end, can be genuinely challenging for the uninitiated.
trivia: Montenegro is named after Princess Elena of Montenegro, who married Italian King Vittorio Emanuele III in 1896 — the liqueur was dedicated to her as a symbol of elegance and refinement.

The recipe for Montenegro uses 40 botanicals, including aromatic herbs from the Euganean Hills, Valdostana gentian, and three varieties of orange peel — the exact proportions are a closely guarded secret.
